<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""86e9700ffc2e1a491088927148ec18cd.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""fa173439c0b165abd62a7936bc2b0ca8.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Configurational&#x20;isomers&#x20;&#x28;differ&#x20;in&#x20;S&#x2F;R&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""313f329985ab18767690aea034c46fbe.png""><br>"	"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Configurational isomers (differ in S/R)</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""313f329985ab18767690aea034c46fbe.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If <b><u>2</u></b> substituents are in the exact <u>same spot</u> but the other <b><u>2 are <i>switched</i></u></b>, they differ by S/R configuration (i.e. configurational isomers).</div><div><br></div><div>This image represents the <b><u>same exact molecule</u></b>...</div><div><img src=""da21f512fc7d308e5e66b473cb114056.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""86e9700ffc2e1a491088927148ec18cd.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Configurational&#x20;isomers&#x20;&#x28;differ&#x20;in&#x20;S&#x2F;R&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""a3b4e53f8d2eb9c04b05c71cbdef714a.png""><br>"	"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Configurational isomers (differ in S/R)</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""a3b4e53f8d2eb9c04b05c71cbdef714a.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If <b><u>2</u></b> substituents are in the exact <u>same spot</u> but the other <b><u>2 are <i>switched</i></u></b>, they differ by S/R configuration (i.e. configurational isomers).</div><div><br></div><div>This image represents the <b><u>same exact molecule</u></b>...</div><div><img src=""da21f512fc7d308e5e66b473cb114056.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""86e9700ffc2e1a491088927148ec18cd.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Configurational&#x20;isomers&#x20;&#x28;differ&#x20;in&#x20;S&#x2F;R&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""deb3a4d2cab43269b49e43503e4a9432.png""><br>"	"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Configurational isomers (differ in S/R)</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""deb3a4d2cab43269b49e43503e4a9432.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If <b><u>2</u></b> substituents are in the exact <u>same spot</u> but the other <b><u>2 are <i>switched</i></u></b>, they differ by S/R configuration (i.e. configurational isomers).</div><div><br></div><div>This image represents the <b><u>same exact molecule</u></b>...</div><div><img src=""da21f512fc7d308e5e66b473cb114056.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""86e9700ffc2e1a491088927148ec18cd.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Same&#x20;exact&#x20;molecule&#x20;&#x28;&lt;u&gt;not&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;different&#x20;by&#x20;S&#x20;or&#x20;R&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""84bf4d6aa3510179962d3bcb9830d52f.png""><br>"	"<div>Are these molecules <i>configurational </i>isomers or the <i>same </i>exact molecule?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Same exact molecule (<u>not</u> different by S or R)</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""84bf4d6aa3510179962d3bcb9830d52f.png""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If <b><u>2</u></b> substituents are in the exact <u>same spot</u> but the other <b><u>2 are <i>switched</i></u></b>, they differ by S/R configuration (i.e. configurational isomers).</div><div><br></div><div>This image represents the <b><u>same exact molecule</u></b>...</div><div><img src=""da21f512fc7d308e5e66b473cb114056.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""86e9700ffc2e1a491088927148ec18cd.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In optical activity, the symbol for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>observed</i></span>&nbsp;rotation is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;α&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>In optical activity, the symbol for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>observed</i></span>&nbsp;rotation is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>α</b></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Specific rotation is [α]<sub>D</sub>. Equation shown below:<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-024aa8576e2630192b79da8ecf4004cdeb08a9c8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b806a2579d20672b92fd6151b137617.png""><br></div></div>"
"Given S or R absolute configuration, you <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> tell which <i>direction </i>plane-polarized light is rotated."	"Given S or R absolute configuration, you <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> tell which <i>direction </i>plane-polarized light is rotated.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>S/R only tells you about the way the atoms are connected around a bond, <b>NOT </b>the direction of plane polarized light (that'd be dextrorotary/levorotary instead).</div><div><br></div><img src=""9dafd6cad68c885dab7e1c1623991d30.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">Meso</b></span> compounds are achiral compounds with an internal <u style="""">plane of symmetry</u>&nbsp;&amp; an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""even"" data-ordinal=""2"">[even/odd]</span> number of chiral centers."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">Meso</b></span> compounds are achiral compounds with an internal <u style="""">plane of symmetry</u>&nbsp;&amp; an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">even</span> number of chiral centers.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Meso compounds are <i>achiral</i>&nbsp;since their even number of chiral centers cancel each other out (equal number of S's to R's).<br></div><div><i><br></i></div><div><img src=""c3e162846d986b27e6577434eb2b4b13.png""><i><br></i></div><div><i><br></i></div><div><img src=""Picture24-2.png""><i><br></i></div></div>"
"In designating S or R absolute configuration, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;reverse&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reverse/do not reverse]</span> the direction when the <u>lowest priority</u> substituent is on a wedge."	"In designating S or R absolute configuration, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>reverse</i></span> the direction when the <u>lowest priority</u> substituent is on a wedge.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Seen on the right.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ae0fc66efa1c46b7a0b613458e30d434.png""><br></div></div>"
"When the <u>lowest priority</u> substituent is on a non-wedge/dash bond but is&nbsp;<i>directly adjacent</i> to the <b>wedge</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;do&#x20;not&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;reverse"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reverse/do not]</span> S/R designation."	"When the <u>lowest priority</u> substituent is on a non-wedge/dash bond but is&nbsp;<i>directly adjacent</i> to the <b>wedge</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">do not </i>reverse</span> S/R designation.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Seen on the <b>left</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""87bf50780e1d8a299867f0bd345321c0.png""></div>"
"When the <u>lowest priority</u> substitient is on a non-wedge/dash bond but is&nbsp;<i>far away</i> from the <b>wedge</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;reverse&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reverse/do not]</span> S/R designation."	"When the <u>lowest priority</u> substitient is on a non-wedge/dash bond but is&nbsp;<i>far away</i> from the <b>wedge</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u><i>reverse</i></u></span> S/R designation.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Seen on the <b>right</b>.</div><div><br></div><div>- Lowest priority is <b><u>close to</u></b> wedge: <b>do not</b> <b>reverse</b>&nbsp;(""stay here"").</div><div>- Lowest priority is <b><u>far</u> </b>away from wedge: <b>reverse</b>&nbsp;(""come here"").<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""87bf50780e1d8a299867f0bd345321c0.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Skeletal&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>isomers </b>are types of <i>constitutional </i>isomers that differ <i>only </i>in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">connectivity of their&nbsp;<u>carbon backbone</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Skeletal</b></span> <b>isomers </b>are types of <i>constitutional </i>isomers that differ <i>only </i>in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">connectivity of their&nbsp;<u>carbon backbone</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""9261ec06f2f3038c64a043e47d046c7e.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Skeletal</b></span> <b>isomers </b>are types of <i>constitutional </i>isomers that differ <i>only </i>in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""connectivity&#x20;of&#x20;their&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;u&gt;carbon&#x20;backbone&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Skeletal</b></span> <b>isomers </b>are types of <i>constitutional </i>isomers that differ <i>only </i>in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">connectivity of their&nbsp;<u>carbon backbone</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""9261ec06f2f3038c64a043e47d046c7e.png""></div>"
"On a chair conformation, compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;cis&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cis/trans]</span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>same </i>up/down direction</span>."	"On a chair conformation, compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>cis</b></span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>same </i>up/down direction</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""f9734683eb19eff4ee053da9f421c4a6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fa173439c0b165abd62a7936bc2b0ca8.png""></div>"
"On a chair conformation, compounds are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>cis</b></span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;same&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;up&#x2F;down&#x20;direction"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"On a chair conformation, compounds are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>cis</b></span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>same </i>up/down direction</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""f9734683eb19eff4ee053da9f421c4a6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fa173439c0b165abd62a7936bc2b0ca8.png""></div>"
"On a <u>chair conformation</u>, compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;trans&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cis/trans]</span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite&nbsp;</i>up/down directions</span>."	"On a <u>chair conformation</u>, compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>trans</b></span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite&nbsp;</i>up/down directions</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""f9734683eb19eff4ee053da9f421c4a6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fa173439c0b165abd62a7936bc2b0ca8.png""></div>"
"On a <u>chair conformation</u>, compounds are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>trans</b></span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;up&#x2F;down&#x20;directions"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"On a <u>chair conformation</u>, compounds are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>trans</b></span> when both of their <u>substituents</u> point in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite&nbsp;</i>up/down directions</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""f9734683eb19eff4ee053da9f421c4a6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""fa173439c0b165abd62a7936bc2b0ca8.png""></div>"
"The bonded atoms in <b>E/Z alkenes</b> <i>are </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;syn&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><u>periplanar</u> to each other, since their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">p-orbitals</span> need to <i>align</i> to form a π bond in the first place."	"The bonded atoms in <b>E/Z alkenes</b> <i>are </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>syn</u></span><u>periplanar</u> to each other, since their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">p-orbitals</span> need to <i>align</i> to form a π bond in the first place.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Since their π bonds align in the first place, they must be within the same place. Otherwise, they wouldn't be able to align.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""main-qimg-1d11cc29192eb69e6ae05a66fe955fb7.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5c6c13489b8e306d7ef7561cfd2a3af1b092485e.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The bonded atoms in <b>E/Z alkenes</b> <i>are </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>syn</u></span><u>periplanar</u> to each other, since their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""p&#x2D;orbitals"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> need to <i>align</i> to form a π bond in the first place."	"The bonded atoms in <b>E/Z alkenes</b> <i>are </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>syn</u></span><u>periplanar</u> to each other, since their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">p-orbitals</span> need to <i>align</i> to form a π bond in the first place.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Since their π bonds align in the first place, they must be within the same place. Otherwise, they wouldn't be able to align.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""main-qimg-1d11cc29192eb69e6ae05a66fe955fb7.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5c6c13489b8e306d7ef7561cfd2a3af1b092485e.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Alkenes</b></span> have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[more/less]</span> <u>entropy</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>alkanes</b></span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Alkenes</b></span> have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>entropy</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>alkanes</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Alkenes <u>lack</u> the ability to freely rotate.</div><div><br></div><div>Alkanes: straight-chain carbons</div><div>Alkenes: double bonds (below)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""609a787b98044bac54038c7eba570bdb.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Alkenes&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[alkanes/alkenes]</span> have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>entropy</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;alkanes&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[alkanes/alkenes]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Alkenes</b></span> have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>entropy</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>alkanes</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Alkenes <u>lack</u> the ability to freely rotate.</div><div><br></div><div>Alkanes: straight-chain carbons</div><div>Alkenes: double bonds (below)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""609a787b98044bac54038c7eba570bdb.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <b>specific rotation</b> of plane-polarized light by an S or R compound is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;physical&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[physical/chemical]</span> property."	"The <b>specific rotation</b> of plane-polarized light by an S or R compound is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>physical</i></span> property.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"Chiral compounds that are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>enanti</u><u>omers</u></span> to one another rotate <b>plane-polarized light</b> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>equal</i></span> magnitudes in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>opposite</i></span> directions.

"Chiral compounds that are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>enanti</u><u>omers</u></span> to one another rotate <b>plane-polarized light</b> with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;equal&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[equal/different]</span> magnitudes in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> directions." "<div>(<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">+</span>)-chiral compounds rotate <b>plane-polarized light</b> in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""clock"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>wise (<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""dextro"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>rotatory) direction.&nbsp;</div>"	"<div>(<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">+</span>)-chiral compounds rotate <b>plane-polarized light</b> in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">clock</span>wise (<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">dextro</span>rotatory) direction.&nbsp;</div>

"(<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">-</span>)-chiral compounds rotate&nbsp;<b>plane-polarized light</b>&nbsp;in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""counter&#x2D;clock"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>wise (<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""levo"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>rotatory) direction." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""bd455967bf1e1fbfd2f59a47a1aaebe5.png""></div>"
"D/L configurations <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> indicate the <u>direction</u> <b>plane-polarized light</b> is rotated by chiral compounds."	"D/L configurations <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">cannot</i></span> indicate the <u>direction</u> <b>plane-polarized light</b> is rotated by chiral compounds.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- D/L only describes the absolute configuration of Fischer projections.</div><div>- <b>d/l</b> <u>can</u> describe optical activity (short for dextrorotatory/levorotatory).</div><div><br></div><img src=""bd455967bf1e1fbfd2f59a47a1aaebe5.png""></div>"
"d/l configurations <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> indicate the direction <b>plane-polarized light</b> is rotated by chiral compounds."	"d/l configurations <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">can</i></span> indicate the direction <b>plane-polarized light</b> is rotated by chiral compounds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- D/L only describes the absolute configuration of Fischer projections.</div><div>- <b>d/l</b> <u>can</u> describe optical activity (short for <b><u>dextrorotatory/levorotatory</u></b>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""bd455967bf1e1fbfd2f59a47a1aaebe5.png""></div>"
"<b>Ethers </b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> form <u>hydrogen bonds</u> with water using the lone pairs on their oxygen atoms."	"<b>Ethers </b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> form <u>hydrogen bonds</u> with water using the lone pairs on their oxygen atoms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ethers <u>cannot</u> form hydrogen bonds between each other though, since their oxygens only contain lone pairs (no hydrogens).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c43889f105964a02797c1397c16dd10.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Ethers </b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> form <u>hydrogen bonds</u> between each other."	"<b>Ethers </b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> form <u>hydrogen bonds</u> between each other.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ethers <u>can</u>&nbsp;form hydrogen bonds with water, since water provides the H.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c43889f105964a02797c1397c16dd10.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Ethers&nbsp;</b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> form&nbsp;<u>hydrogen bonds</u>&nbsp;with water using lone pairs on their oxygen atoms."	"<b>Ethers&nbsp;</b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> form&nbsp;<u>hydrogen bonds</u>&nbsp;with water using lone pairs on their oxygen atoms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Esters cannot hydrogen bond with only each other though, since they lack a hydrogen atom.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8aa8a90c4df6ad37d478187e6bf79d18.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Ethers&nbsp;</b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> form&nbsp;<u>hydrogen bonds</u>&nbsp;with each other."	"<b>Ethers&nbsp;</b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> form&nbsp;<u>hydrogen bonds</u>&nbsp;with each other.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since they lack a hydrogen atom. They <u>can</u> hydrogen bond with water, since water provides the hydrogen &amp; they provide the lone pair.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8aa8a90c4df6ad37d478187e6bf79d18.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Electrocyclic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>reactions</b> are <u>pericyclic</u> reactions where rings either <i>form </i>or <i>break apart</i> - converting a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">π-bond</span> to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ-bond</span> (or vice versa).</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Electrocyclic</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are <u>pericyclic</u> reactions where rings either <i>form </i>or <i>break apart</i> - converting a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">π-bond</span> to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ-bond</span> (or vice versa).</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Since these are pericyclic reactions, this reaction is concerted (all in <u>one step</u>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""d9846f4709d6f831e89d25c3297ddf6a.png""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Electrocyclic</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are <u>pericyclic</u> reactions where rings either <i>form </i>or <i>break apart</i> - converting a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""π&#x2D;bond"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> to a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""σ&#x2D;bond"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> (or vice versa).</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Electrocyclic</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are <u>pericyclic</u> reactions where rings either <i>form </i>or <i>break apart</i> - converting a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">π-bond</span> to a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ-bond</span> (or vice versa).</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Since these are pericyclic reactions, this reaction is concerted (all in <u>one step</u>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""d9846f4709d6f831e89d25c3297ddf6a.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Initiation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> reactions are those where bonds are broken <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">homo</span>lytically using UV light or peroxides to <i>increase</i>&nbsp;the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>free radicals</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Initiation</b></span> reactions are those where bonds are broken <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">homo</span>lytically using UV light or peroxides to <i>increase</i>&nbsp;the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>free radicals</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- UV light is needed to overcome the energy needed to do this.</div><div>- Homolytically because each bond shares electrons equally - hence creating the free radical.</div><div><br></div><img src=""11add4c9a1391d1d92337e84c53f9ce1.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Initiation</b></span> reactions are those where bonds are broken <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""homo"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>lytically using UV light or peroxides to <i>increase</i>&nbsp;the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>free radicals</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Initiation</b></span> reactions are those where bonds are broken <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">homo</span>lytically using UV light or peroxides to <i>increase</i>&nbsp;the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>free radicals</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- UV light is needed to overcome the energy needed to do this.</div><div>- Homolytically because each bond shares electrons equally - hence creating the free radical.</div><div><br></div><img src=""11add4c9a1391d1d92337e84c53f9ce1.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Initiation</b></span> reactions are those where bonds are broken <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">homo</span>lytically using UV light or peroxides to <i>increase</i>&nbsp;the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;free&#x20;radicals&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Initiation</b></span> reactions are those where bonds are broken <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">homo</span>lytically using UV light or peroxides to <i>increase</i>&nbsp;the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>free radicals</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- UV light is needed to overcome the energy needed to do this.</div><div>- Homolytically because each bond shares electrons equally - hence creating the free radical.</div><div><br></div><img src=""11add4c9a1391d1d92337e84c53f9ce1.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Propagation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> reactions are those where there is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>no net change</i></span> in the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>free radicals</u></span> from the reactants → products."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Propagation</b></span> reactions are those where there is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>no net change</i></span> in the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>free radicals</u></span> from the reactants → products.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Propagation</b></span> reactions are those where there is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;no&#x20;net&#x20;change&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;free&#x20;radicals&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> from the reactants → products."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Propagation</b></span> reactions are those where there is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>no net change</i></span> in the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>free radicals</u></span> from the reactants → products.

<div># free radicals in reactants = # free radicals in products</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3-first-propagation-step-for-free-radical-chlorination-chlorine-radical-abstracts-hydrogen-atom-from-ch4.gif""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Termination&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> reactions are those where the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>free radicals</u></span> <i>decreases</i> from the reactants → products."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Termination</b></span> reactions are those where the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>free radicals</u></span> <i>decreases</i> from the reactants → products.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b><u>Loses</u></b> free radicals.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6e2a6893548412913c49eef45a24caa3.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Termination</b></span> reactions are those where the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;free&#x20;radicals&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>decreases</i> from the reactants → products."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Termination</b></span> reactions are those where the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>free radicals</u></span> <i>decreases</i> from the reactants → products.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b><u>Loses</u></b> free radicals.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6e2a6893548412913c49eef45a24caa3.png""><br></div></div>"

"1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">π-bond</span> has <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">2</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">π-electrons</span>.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Markovnikov</span><b> addition</b> reactions add <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;halides&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[hydrogens/halides]</span> to the side of the π-bond with the <i style="""">most</i> substituents."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Markovnikov</span><b> addition</b> reactions add <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>halides</u></span> to the side of the π-bond with the <i style="""">most</i> substituents.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Markovnikov: halide goes to most-substituted spot.</div><div>- Anti-Markovnikov: halide goes to least substituted spot.</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3fc19671710adac461e74916b01a9cf7.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Markovnikov"" data-ordinal=""2"">[Mark/anti-Mark]</span><b> addition</b> reactions add <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>halides</u></span> to the side of the π-bond with the <i style="""">most</i> substituents."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Markovnikov</span><b> addition</b> reactions add <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>halides</u></span> to the side of the π-bond with the <i style="""">most</i> substituents.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Markovnikov: halide goes to most-substituted spot.</div><div>- Anti-Markovnikov: halide goes to least substituted spot.</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3fc19671710adac461e74916b01a9cf7.png""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Dividing by the length and concentration of the sample <u>standardizes</u> the conditions (hence what specific rotation even is).</div><div>- Can be used in the below equation to find <b>enantiomeric excess</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""1b806a2579d20672b92fd6151b137617.png""><br></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Diastereomers&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[enant/diast]</span> <i>always </i>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <b>physical&nbsp;</b>&amp;<b> chemical</b><b>&nbsp;properties</b>, including density, boiling point, melting point, reactivity, etc.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Diastereomers</u></span> <i>always </i>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <b>physical&nbsp;</b>&amp;<b> chemical</b><b>&nbsp;properties</b>, including density, boiling point, melting point, reactivity, etc.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Density, boiling point, melting point, solubilities will be different.<div>- This is why diastereomers can be separated by fractional distillation, extraction, etc.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;Enantiomers&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[enant/diast]</span>&nbsp;<i>always&nbsp;</i>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>the same</i></span> <u>physical</u>&nbsp;&amp; <u>chemical</u>&nbsp;properties, including density, boiling point, melting point, reactivity, etc."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b style="""">Enantiomers</b></span>&nbsp;<i>always&nbsp;</i>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>the same</i></span> <u>physical</u>&nbsp;&amp; <u>chemical</u>&nbsp;properties, including density, boiling point, melting point, reactivity, etc.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Diastereomers have different physical/chemical properties.</div>"
"<div><b>Meso </b>compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;most&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[least/most]</span> stable when they have an <u>inversion point</u> - analogous to each side being ""trans"" to the other.</div>"	"<div><b>Meso </b>compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>most</i></span> stable when they have an <u>inversion point</u> - analogous to each side being ""trans"" to the other.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Experience less steric hinderance from large groups being synperiplanar to each other... gives them a little more room.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c3e162846d986b27e6577434eb2b4b13.png""></div>"
"<div><b>Meso </b>compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;least&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[least/most]</span> stable when each side is a perfect mirror image of the other.</div>"	"<div><b>Meso </b>compounds are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>least</i></span> stable when each side is a perfect mirror image of the other.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Analogous to each side being ""cis"" to the other... increases steric hinderance.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c3e162846d986b27e6577434eb2b4b13.png""></div>"
"A <b>meso </b>compound and its <i>mirror image</i> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;identical&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[enant/diast/same]</span> to each other."	"A <b>meso </b>compound and its <i>mirror image</i> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>identical</u></span> to each other.
<hr>

"<div>In <b>Newman projections</b>, groups in the <u>plane</u> of the molecule (no wedge or dash) are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""up&#x20;or&#x20;down"" data-ordinal=""1"">[direction]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>In <b>Newman projections</b>, groups in the <u>plane</u> of the molecule (no wedge or dash) are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">up or down</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>True from looking at either the left or the right.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ee2f95f640c120160c9de8db260dab5c.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""a9053fd8ab14021017beb6750b32b3ed.png""></div>"
"<b>Cycloalkanes </b>with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""3"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""4"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> carbons are the <i>most </i>reactive due to their high amounts of <u>ring strain</u> and bond instability."	"<b>Cycloalkanes </b>with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">3</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">4</span> carbons are the <i>most </i>reactive due to their high amounts of <u>ring strain</u> and bond instability.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""2abf38ad40ff341f46b4c9aae18dc7a1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The electron density in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;cyclopropane&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cycloalkane]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> lie between its two nuclei - weakening the bonds &amp; making it more reactive."	"The electron density in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>cyclopropane</b></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> lie between its two nuclei - weakening the bonds &amp; making it more reactive.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Normally, ~80% of the electron densities in σ bonds lie between the two nuclei... not in this case though (bows outwards).<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2abf38ad40ff341f46b4c9aae18dc7a1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The electron density in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>cyclopropane</b></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[does/does not]</span> lie between its two nuclei - weakening the bonds &amp; making it more reactive."	"The electron density in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>cyclopropane</b></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>does not</i></span> lie between its two nuclei - weakening the bonds &amp; making it more reactive.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Normally, ~80% of the electron densities in σ bonds lie between the two nuclei... not in this case though (bows outwards).<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2abf38ad40ff341f46b4c9aae18dc7a1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Cyclopentane&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cycloalkane]</span> <i>avoids </i><u>torsional strain</u> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">eclipsing hydrogens</span> by <i>puckering out</i> one of its carbon atoms."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Cyclopentane</b></span> <i>avoids </i><u>torsional strain</u> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">eclipsing hydrogens</span> by <i>puckering out</i> one of its carbon atoms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Puckering also helps make its bond angles even closer to 109.5 (was 108 before puckering).</div><div>- Even after puckering, other carbons still have eclipsing hydrogens (which is why cyclopentane is not as stable as cyclohexane). Mostly diminishes it.<br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x5SdUOTivj8"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x5SdUOTivj8</a></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0feae797523f9b2f263a707cc17e76c4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""a835cc189b12337392027042f601f0b8.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Cyclopentane</b></span> <i>avoids </i><u>torsional strain</u> from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""eclipsing&#x20;hydrogens"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> by <i>puckering out</i> one of its carbon atoms."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Cyclopentane</b></span> <i>avoids </i><u>torsional strain</u> from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">eclipsing hydrogens</span> by <i>puckering out</i> one of its carbon atoms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Puckering also helps make its bond angles even closer to 109.5 (was 108 before puckering).</div><div>- Even after puckering, other carbons still have eclipsing hydrogens (which is why cyclopentane is not as stable as cyclohexane). Mostly diminishes it.<br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x5SdUOTivj8"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x5SdUOTivj8</a></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0feae797523f9b2f263a707cc17e76c4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""a835cc189b12337392027042f601f0b8.png""></div>"
"<u>Before</u> ""puckering"" a carbon out of the plane, <b>cyclopentane</b>'s bond angles are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""108&#xB0;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[degrees]</span>."	"<u>Before</u> ""puckering"" a carbon out of the plane, <b>cyclopentane</b>'s bond angles are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">108°</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Puckering both reduces bond angle strain &amp; makes it even closer to 109.5.</div><div>- Even after puckering, other carbons still have eclipsing hydrogens (which is why cyclopentane is not as stable as cyclohexane). Mostly diminishes it.<br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x5SdUOTivj8"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x5SdUOTivj8</a></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0feae797523f9b2f263a707cc17e76c4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""a835cc189b12337392027042f601f0b8.png""></div>"
"<b>Cyclohexane&nbsp;</b>is&nbsp;<i>most</i> stable in its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""chair&#x20;conformation"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Cyclohexane&nbsp;</b>is&nbsp;<i>most</i> stable in its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">chair conformation</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The two different chair conformations can interconvert through chair flips. These flips happen by forming the boat conformation as an intermediate.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""67dec8064aea9e46de93765b782b3318.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Cyclohexane </b><i>interconverts </i>between its different <u>chair conformations</u> by forming <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""boat&#x20;conformations"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> as <i>intermediates</i>."	"<b>Cyclohexane </b><i>interconverts </i>between its different <u>chair conformations</u> by forming <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">boat conformations</span> as <i>intermediates</i>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The two different chair conformations can interconvert through chair flips.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""67dec8064aea9e46de93765b782b3318.png""><br></div></div>"
"Substituents on the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;1&#x2C;3&#x2D;diaxial&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> position on a <u>chair conformation</u> have the <i>greatest </i>amount of repulsion/steric hindernace."	"Substituents on the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>1,3-diaxial</b></span> position on a <u>chair conformation</u> have the <i>greatest </i>amount of repulsion/steric hindernace.

"The oxygen atom on<b> carbonyls</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[more/less]</span> <u>electron withdrawing</u> than the oxygen on <b>alcohols</b>."	"The oxygen atom on<b> carbonyls</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>electron withdrawing</u> than the oxygen on <b>alcohols</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""a90ee9554f4481fe742aee03face1ea6.png""></div>"
"<b>Aldehydes</b> &amp; <b>ketones </b>have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[higher/lower]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">boiling points</span> than regular alkanes due to the <i>increased </i><u>intermolecular attractions</u> incuded by their dipoles."	"<b>Aldehydes</b> &amp; <b>ketones </b>have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">boiling points</span> than regular alkanes due to the <i>increased </i><u>intermolecular attractions</u> incuded by their dipoles.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- They cannot hydrogen bond though, so they <u>don't</u> elevate the boiling point as high as alcohols do.</div><div><br></div><img src=""a90ee9554f4481fe742aee03face1ea6.png""></div>"
"<b>Aldehydes</b> &amp; <b>ketones </b>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""boiling&#x20;points"" data-ordinal=""2"">[physical property]</span> than regular alkanes due to the <i>increased </i><u>intermolecular attractions</u> incuded by their dipoles."	"<b>Aldehydes</b> &amp; <b>ketones </b>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">boiling points</span> than regular alkanes due to the <i>increased </i><u>intermolecular attractions</u> incuded by their dipoles.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- They cannot hydrogen bond though, so they <u>don't</u> elevate the boiling point as high as alcohols do.</div><div><br></div><img src=""a90ee9554f4481fe742aee03face1ea6.png""></div>"
"<div><b>Aldehydes/ketones</b> increase <u>boiling point</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[more/less]</span> than <b>alcohols</b>.</div>"	"<div><b>Aldehydes/ketones</b> increase <u>boiling point</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> than <b>alcohols</b>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Aldehydes/ketones have stronger intermolecular interactions, but alcohols are also capable of hydrogen bonding.</div><div><br></div><img src=""a90ee9554f4481fe742aee03face1ea6.png""></div>"
"Primary&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>aldehydes</b></span> with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;pyridinium&#x20;chlorochromate&#x20;&#x28;PCC&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reagent]</span>."	"Primary&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>aldehydes</b></span> with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC)</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Stronger oxidants lead to the formation of a <u>carboxylic acid</u> instead.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-002c627c52952403927a285d89e5f096a32665cd.png""><br></div></div>"
"Primary&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;aldehydes&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC)</u></span>."	"Primary&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>aldehydes</b></span> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC)</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Stronger oxidants lead to the formation of a <u>carboxylic acid</u> instead.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-002c627c52952403927a285d89e5f096a32665cd.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Secondary"" data-ordinal=""1"">[1°/2°/3°]</span>&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ketones</b></span> using oxidants of any strength (PCC, Na<sub>2</sub>Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>7</sub>, etc.)."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Secondary</span>&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ketones</b></span> using oxidants of any strength (PCC, Na<sub>2</sub>Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>7</sub>, etc.).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Secondary</span>&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ketones&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> using oxidants of any strength (PCC, Na<sub>2</sub>Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>7</sub>, etc.)."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Secondary</span>&nbsp;<b>alcohols&nbsp;</b>can be <i>oxidized </i>into <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ketones</b></span> using oxidants of any strength (PCC, Na<sub>2</sub>Cr<sub>2</sub>O<sub>7</sub>, etc.).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"Attacks from nucleophiles on <b>carbonyls</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;without&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[with/without]</span> good <u>leaving groups</u> will ""open"" the carbonyl, become&nbsp;<i>reduced</i>&nbsp;to an alcohol, and add the nucleophile."	"Attacks from nucleophiles on <b>carbonyls</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>without</i></span> good <u>leaving groups</u> will ""open"" the carbonyl, become&nbsp;<i>reduced</i>&nbsp;to an alcohol, and add the nucleophile.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is what happens to <b><u>aldehydes</u> </b>&amp; <b><u>ketones</u> </b>- nucleophilic attack results in carbonyls that are unable to reform after being attacked.</div><div>- If it did have a good leaving group (like an ester), the carbonyl will reform itself after getting attacked (will not reduce to an OH).</div><div><br></div><img src=""dabc770cdb28dcee166da1d478009d0d.png""></div>"
"Attacks from nucleophiles on&nbsp;<b>carbonyls</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;with&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[with/without]</span> good&nbsp;<u>leaving groups</u>&nbsp;will ""open"" the carbonyl, add the nucleophile, and reform itself <i>back into</i> a carbonyl."	"Attacks from nucleophiles on&nbsp;<b>carbonyls</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>with</i></span> good&nbsp;<u>leaving groups</u>&nbsp;will ""open"" the carbonyl, add the nucleophile, and reform itself <i>back into</i> a carbonyl.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is what happens to <b style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">carboxylic acids</b>...&nbsp;nucleophile gets ""added"" onto the carbonyl carbon while the water group leaves - carbonyl reforms.</div><div>- If it did have a good leaving group (like an ester), the carbonyl will reform itself after getting attacked (will not reduce to an OH).</div><div><br></div><img src=""image203.jpg""><br></div>"
"<div>This represents a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hydration"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> reaction (nucleophilic addition).</div><div><br></div><img src=""45c938a7cb878051f03c19017b616bfb.png"">"	"<div>This represents a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">hydration</span> reaction (nucleophilic addition).</div><div><br></div><img src=""45c938a7cb878051f03c19017b616bfb.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Hydration&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>reactions </b>are <u>nucleophilic addition</u> reactions where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span> <i>attacks </i>the carbonyl of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">aldehydes</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">ketones</span> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>geminal diols</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hydration</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are <u>nucleophilic addition</u> reactions where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span> <i>attacks </i>the carbonyl of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">aldehydes</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">ketones</span> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>geminal diols</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Geminal diol</b>: two OH groups are attached to the same carbon.</div><div><br></div><img src=""45c938a7cb878051f03c19017b616bfb.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hydration</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are <u>nucleophilic addition</u> reactions where <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>attacks </i>the carbonyl of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">aldehydes</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">ketones</span> to form <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;geminal&#x20;diols&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hydration</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are <u>nucleophilic addition</u> reactions where <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span> <i>attacks </i>the carbonyl of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">aldehydes</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">ketones</span> to form <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>geminal diols</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Geminal diol</b>: two OH groups are attached to the same carbon.</div><div><br></div><img src=""45c938a7cb878051f03c19017b616bfb.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hydration</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are <u>nucleophilic addition</u> reactions where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span> <i>attacks </i>the carbonyl of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""aldehydes"" data-ordinal=""3"">[group]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ketones"" data-ordinal=""3"">[group]</span> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>geminal diols</u></span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Geminal&#x20;diols&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are groups where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>two </i>alcohols</span> are attached to<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">the&nbsp;<i>same&nbsp;</i>carbon</span>.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Geminal diols</b></span> are groups where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>two </i>alcohols</span> are attached to<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">the&nbsp;<i>same&nbsp;</i>carbon</span>.</div>

"The rates of <b>hydration</b>&nbsp;<b>reactions </b>(nucleophilic addition) can be <i>sped up</i> by adding a small amount of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;catalytic&#x20;acid&#x2F;base&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The rates of <b>hydration</b>&nbsp;<b>reactions </b>(nucleophilic addition) can be <i>sped up</i> by adding a small amount of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>catalytic acid/base</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Works with either acids or bases.</div><div>- ""Catalytic"" refers to the fact that <u>not much</u> is needed.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""45c938a7cb878051f03c19017b616bfb.png""><br></div></div>"
"Treating an <b>aldehyde</b> or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;alcohols&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;basic&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acidic/basic]</span> solution results in the formation of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">hemiacetals/hemiketals</span>."	"Treating an <b>aldehyde</b> or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>basic</i></span> solution results in the formation of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">hemiacetals/hemiketals</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If the solution was <u>acidic</u>, it would continue to react to form an <b>acetal</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""b7bc5a4a596c2bbcc5211ab1fae3e034.png""></div>"
"Treating an <b>aldehyde</b> or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>basic</i></span> solution results in the formation of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hemiacetals&#x2F;hemiketals"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"Treating an <b>aldehyde</b> or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>basic</i></span> solution results in the formation of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">hemiacetals/hemiketals</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If the solution was <u>acidic</u>, it would continue to react to form an <b>acetal</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""b7bc5a4a596c2bbcc5211ab1fae3e034.png""></div>"
"Treating an <b>aldehyde </b>or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;alcohols&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;acidic&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acidic/basic]</span> conditions first forms a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">hemiacetal/hemiketal</span>, then reacts to form an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">acetal/ketal</span>."	"Treating an <b>aldehyde </b>or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acidic</i></span> conditions first forms a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">hemiacetal/hemiketal</span>, then reacts to form an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">acetal/ketal</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Acidic conditions are needed because an acid is needed to protonate the alcohol group - forming water as a leaving group. Water leaves, and then another alcohol group attacks.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9762e34d8dcdf86be3f0ab2c4aaa29a5.png""></div>"
"Treating an <b>aldehyde </b>or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acidic</i></span> conditions first forms a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hemiacetal&#x2F;hemiketal"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, then reacts to form an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""acetal&#x2F;ketal"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"Treating an <b>aldehyde </b>or <b>ketone </b>with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acidic</i></span> conditions first forms a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">hemiacetal/hemiketal</span>, then reacts to form an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">acetal/ketal</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Acidic conditions are needed because an acid is needed to protonate the alcohol group - forming water as a leaving group. Water leaves, and then another alcohol group attacks.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9762e34d8dcdf86be3f0ab2c4aaa29a5.png""></div>"
"<div><b>Acetal </b>formation requires an <u>acidic</u> environment in order for an acid to protonate a hemiacetal's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;first&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;OH"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - causing it to leave &amp; allowing a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;second&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;alcohol"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to attack.</div>"	"<div><b>Acetal </b>formation requires an <u>acidic</u> environment in order for an acid to protonate a hemiacetal's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>first </i>OH</span>&nbsp;to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span> - causing it to leave &amp; allowing a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>second </i>alcohol</span> to attack.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Same</u> exact process with <b>hemiketals </b>→ <b>ketals</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""9762e34d8dcdf86be3f0ab2c4aaa29a5.png""><br></div></div>"
"The formation of <b>acetals/ketals</b> <i>from </i><u>hemiacetals/hemiketals</u> proceeds as an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""S&lt;sub&gt;N&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S<sub>N</sub>1/S<sub>N</sub>2]</span> nucleophilic substitution." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<b>NaBH<sub>4</sub></b> acts as a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[strong/weak]</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reducing&#x20;agent"" data-ordinal=""1"">[ox./red. agent]</span>." <hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Can only reduce <b>aldehydes</b>,&nbsp;<b>ketones</b>, &amp; <b>acid chlorides</b> to alcohol groups.</div><div>- <u>Cannot</u> reduce carboxylic acids, esters, amides, or nitriles.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5db941759a6cb22c2397c804c21eee0e.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <b>α-carbon</b> adjacent to a carbonyl is <i>highly </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;acidic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> due to both the <i>weakness</i> of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">C-H bonds</span> and its highly stable <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">conjugate base (enolate)</span>."	"The <b>α-carbon</b> adjacent to a carbonyl is <i>highly </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>acidic</u></span> due to both the <i>weakness</i> of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">C-H bonds</span> and its highly stable <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">conjugate base (enolate)</span>.

"<b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ketones"" data-ordinal=""1"">[ald/ket]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <u>acidic</u>&nbsp;than the <b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""aldehydes"" data-ordinal=""1"">[ald/ket]</span>."	"<b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ketones</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <u>acidic</u>&nbsp;than the <b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">aldehydes</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because the alkyl group on the ketone is electron donating.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c23daf926c60aef6c4f7594a92bc7c73.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ketones</span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[more/less]</span> <u>acidic</u>&nbsp;than the <b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">aldehydes</span>."	"<b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ketones</span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <u>acidic</u>&nbsp;than the <b>α-carbons</b> adjacent to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">aldehydes</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because the alkyl group on the ketone is electron donating.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c23daf926c60aef6c4f7594a92bc7c73.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Enolates</b> are <i>highly </i>stable due to their ability to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;stabilize&#x20;the&amp;nbsp&#x3B;carbanion&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;through its resonance structure."	"<b>Enolates</b> are <i>highly </i>stable due to their ability to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>stabilize the&nbsp;carbanion</u></span>&nbsp;through its resonance structure.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Remember, <u>negative</u> charges should be stabilized on the most <b>electronegative </b>atom.</div><div>- Electron donating groups <u>destabilize</u> this carbanion.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""61bd2d18dd2d7e4d7681d02476f15a43.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c23daf926c60aef6c4f7594a92bc7c73.png""><br></div></div>"
"Electron <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;withdrawing&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[donating/with.]</span> groups <i>stabilize </i><b>carbanions</b>, while electron <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;donating&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[donating/with.]</span> groups <i>destabilize</i> <b>carbanions</b>."	"Electron <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>withdrawing</u></span> groups <i>stabilize </i><b>carbanions</b>, while electron <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>donating</u></span> groups <i>destabilize</i> <b>carbanions</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Anythng that helps pull the electron density off of the carbon will stabilize it.</div><div><br></div><div>- Remember, <u>negative</u> charges should be stabilized on the most <b>electronegative </b>atom.</div><div>- This is why carbanions are most stable on <u>primary</u> carbons.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8cd368e4acf73beddfe84ca6fb247fb26bd68554.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In <b>keto-enol</b> tautomerization, the <u>equilibrium</u> heavily favors the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ketone"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In <b>keto-enol</b> tautomerization, the <u>equilibrium</u> heavily favors the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ketone</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Aldol condensation</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fdfe11123512131f9d1b1c843f91b96107b5e462.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Why is a <b>monocarboxylic acid</b> <i>more </i><u>acidic</u> than a dicarboxylic acid with only 1 group deprotonated?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""If&#x20;the&#x20;dicarboxylic&#x20;acid&#x20;lost&#x20;its&#x20;other&#x20;proton&#x2C;&#x20;the&#x20;two&#x20;negatively&#x20;charged&#x20;carboxylate&#x20;groups&#x20;would&#x20;cause&#x20;extreme&#x20;instability&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Why is a <b>monocarboxylic acid</b> <i>more </i><u>acidic</u> than a dicarboxylic acid with only 1 group deprotonated?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">If the dicarboxylic acid lost its other proton, the two negatively charged carboxylate groups would cause extreme instability.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""mfcd00002707-medium.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;β&#x2D;dicarboxylic&#x20;acids&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are dicarboxylic acids separated by only one single α-carbon."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>β-dicarboxylic acids</b></span> are dicarboxylic acids separated by only one single α-carbon.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The <b>OH's </b>on each carboxylic acid group are <u>more acidic than the α-carbon</u>, but it's worth noting that the α-carbon here is very acidic as well.</div><div><br></div><img src=""02f28512536d24039439e597bacb0708.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8843920597035be1157530630c246067.png""><br></div></div>"
"On a&nbsp;<b>β-dicarboxylic acid, </b>the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""OH"" data-ordinal=""1"">[α/OH]</span> group is the <u><i>most</i> acidic</u>."	"On a&nbsp;<b>β-dicarboxylic acid, </b>the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">OH</span> group is the <u><i>most</i> acidic</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The <b>OH's </b>on each carboxylic acid group are <u>more acidic than the α-carbon</u>, but it's worth noting that the α-carbon here is very acidic as well (due to resonance stability).</div><div><br></div><img src=""02f28512536d24039439e597bacb0708.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8843920597035be1157530630c246067.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>This <b>carboxylic acid</b> underwent a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""nucleophilic&#x20;acyl&#x20;substitution"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9586af90822b12375c46fd750b9b1e08.png"">"	"<div>This <b>carboxylic acid</b> underwent a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">nucleophilic acyl substitution</span> reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9586af90822b12375c46fd750b9b1e08.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Nucleophile (likely an acyl-carrying group) attacked the carbonyl of the carboxylic acid - adding the nucleophile, kicking off the leaving group, and re-forming the carboxylic acid.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""45cabc8a66fe77ab546c98b2247dd814.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>This is called a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""acyl"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> group.</div><div><br></div><img src=""34181174ed0460f570c4daa9266d3d11.png"">"	"<div>This is called a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">acyl</span> group.</div><div><br></div><img src=""34181174ed0460f570c4daa9266d3d11.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Formed through nucleophilic acyl substitutions of <b>carboxylic acids</b>.</div><div>- Nucleophile (likely an acyl-carrying group) attacked the carbonyl of the carboxylic acid - adding the nucleophile, kicking off the leaving group, and re-forming the carboxylic acid.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""45cabc8a66fe77ab546c98b2247dd814.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><b>Acyl</b> groups are <i>derived </i>from substitution reactions on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""carboxylic&#x20;acids"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div><b>Acyl</b> groups are <i>derived </i>from substitution reactions on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">carboxylic acids</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Nucleophile (likely an acyl-carrying group) attacked the carbonyl of the carboxylic acid - adding the nucleophile, kicking off the leaving group, and re-forming the carboxylic acid.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""34181174ed0460f570c4daa9266d3d11.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""45cabc8a66fe77ab546c98b2247dd814.png""><br></div></div>"

"<u>Carboxylic acids</u> are converted into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>amides</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">R-NH<sub>2</sub></span> <i>nucleophilically attacks</i> in a(n) <u>acidic/basic</u>&nbsp;solution.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Fischer&#x20;esterification&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>reactions</b> are when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span>&nbsp;react with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohols</u></span> in acidic solutions to form <b>esters</b>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fischer esterification</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span>&nbsp;react with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohols</u></span> in acidic solutions to form <b>esters</b>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Acid catalysis helps speed this up because it protonates the carbonyl oxygen - making it more electrophilic.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c37f315f0df8112a7f5c651e7d33448b7a3b0aa0.jpg""><br></div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fischer esterification</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span>&nbsp;react with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohols</u></span> in acidic solutions to form <b>esters</b>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Acidic </b>solution: Carbonyl oxygen gets protonated by the acidic solution, making the carbonyl carbon even more electrophilic (making it easier for H<sub>2</sub>O to attack).</div><div>- Basic solution: OH<sup>-</sup> nucleophilically attacks the carbonyl carbon (as opposed to protonating the carbonyl oxygen).</div><div><br></div><img src=""37b1f68392c84aeb6f3982ed52322191.png""></div>"
"All <b>amino acids</b> are <u>chiral</u> except for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""glycine"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"All <b>amino acids</b> are <u>chiral</u> except for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">glycine</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- All amino acids are S in absolute configuration, except for cysteine (R).</div>"

"In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, <u>infared light</u> is shone through samples of compounds in order to cause molecules to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;vibrate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in different ways, depending on their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""functional&#x20;groups"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, <u>infared light</u> is shone through samples of compounds in order to cause molecules to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>vibrate</i></span> in different ways, depending on their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">functional groups</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Different functional groups absorb IR light differently.</div><div><br></div><img src=""3c719375f1b75692740ba15c6486b8d7.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""80b27b2c3bce88d071f97cf63a01ca90.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>IR spectroscopy</b></span>, the <u>covalent bonds</u> of molecules can <i>vibrate </i>in 4 different ways: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""symmetric&#x20;&lt;u&gt;bends&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""asymmetric&#x20;&lt;u&gt;bends&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""symmetric&#x20;&lt;u&gt;stretches&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""asymmetric&#x20;&lt;u&gt;stretches&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>IR spectroscopy</b></span>, the <u>covalent bonds</u> of molecules can <i>vibrate </i>in 4 different ways: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">symmetric <u>bends</u></span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">asymmetric <u>bends</u></span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">symmetric <u>stretches</u></span>, &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">asymmetric <u>stretches</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is why <b>infared light</b> is used... because this type of light causes molecules to <u>vibrate/contract</u> their covalent bonds in unique ways depending on energy absorbance &amp; their functional groups.</div><div><br></div><img src=""3c719375f1b75692740ba15c6486b8d7.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""80b27b2c3bce88d071f97cf63a01ca90.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;IR&#x20;spectroscopy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[spectroscopy]</span>, the <u>covalent bonds</u> of molecules can <i>vibrate </i>in 4 different ways: <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">symmetric <u>bends</u></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">asymmetric <u>bends</u></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">symmetric <u>stretches</u></span>, &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">asymmetric <u>stretches</u></span>."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>IR spectroscopy</b></span>, the <u>covalent bonds</u> of molecules can <i>vibrate </i>in 4 different ways: <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">symmetric <u>bends</u></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">asymmetric <u>bends</u></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">symmetric <u>stretches</u></span>, &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">asymmetric <u>stretches</u></span>.

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Infared&#x20;&#x28;IR&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>spectroscopy </b>uses <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">wavenumbers (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</span>&nbsp;to measure the <u>absorption frequencies</u> of different molecules."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Infared (IR)</b></span> <b>spectroscopy </b>uses <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">wavenumbers (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</span>&nbsp;to measure the <u>absorption frequencies</u> of different molecules.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Infared (IR)</b></span> <b>spectroscopy </b>uses <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">wavenumbers (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</span>&nbsp;to measure the <u>absorption frequencies</u> of different molecules.
