<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Don't even bother trying to interpret anything from this region. Only experts can do that.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, a moleucle's <u>absorption</u> can be recorded <i>only </i>when there's a change in its bond's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""dipole&#x20;moment"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, a moleucle's <u>absorption</u> can be recorded <i>only </i>when there's a change in its bond's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">dipole moment</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Stretching/bending changes the ""shape"" of the covalent bonds in a way that can be detectable. If those dipole moments are undetectable, you'd have no way of knowing if it was bending/stretching.</div><div>- This means molecules with the exact same electronegativities or perfectly symmetrical molecules are <u>undetectable</u>&nbsp;(no change in their dipole momemnts).<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3c719375f1b75692740ba15c6486b8d7.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;IR&#x20;spectroscopy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[spectroscopy]</span>, molecules with <i>equal</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativities</span> or <i>perfect&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">symmetry</span> are <u>undetectable</u> since they can't produce noticable dipole moments.</div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>IR spectroscopy</b></span>, molecules with <i>equal</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativities</span> or <i>perfect&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">symmetry</span> are <u>undetectable</u> since they can't produce noticable dipole moments.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Vibrational movements (stretching/bending) are useful for this because it changes the ""shape"" of&nbsp; the covalent bonding - inducing dipole moments.</div><div>- Absorption can only be detected when changes in their dipole moments can be detected.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3c719375f1b75692740ba15c6486b8d7.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>IR spectroscopy</b></span>, molecules with <i>equal</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""electronegativities"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> or <i>perfect&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""symmetry"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> are <u>undetectable</u> since they can't produce noticable dipole moments.</div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>IR spectroscopy</b></span>, molecules with <i>equal</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativities</span> or <i>perfect&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">symmetry</span> are <u>undetectable</u> since they can't produce noticable dipole moments.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Vibrational movements (stretching/bending) are useful for this because it changes the ""shape"" of&nbsp; the covalent bonding - inducing dipole moments.</div><div>- Absorption can only be detected when changes in their dipole moments can be detected.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3c719375f1b75692740ba15c6486b8d7.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;alcohols&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[func. group]</span> have <i>broad </i>absorbances around <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">3100<sup>-1</sup></span>&nbsp;to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">3500cm<sup>-1</sup></span>." "In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">transmittance</span> refers to the amount of infared light that passes through the sample and reaches the detector.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Different functional groups on molecules absorb this infared light to different degrees.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""wavenumber&#x20;&#x28;cm&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;1&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is plotted against <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x25;&#x20;transmittance"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In <b>IR spectroscopy</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">wavenumber (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</span> is plotted against <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">% transmittance</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;UV&#x2D;Vis&#x20;spectroscopy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[spectroscopy]</span> is <i>most</i> useful to study molecules with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conjugated π bonds</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV-Vis spectroscopy</b></span> is <i>most</i> useful to study molecules with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conjugated π bonds</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because delocalized π electrons can be excited to higher energy levels when treated with UV light.<br></div><div>- Absorption to higher energy levels causes a change in color, which is useful for detecting changes in chemical reactions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""uv-vis-faq-instrument-design-spectrophotometer-function.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV-Vis spectroscopy</b></span> is <i>most</i> useful to study molecules with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;conjugated&#x20;π&#x20;bonds&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV-Vis spectroscopy</b></span> is <i>most</i> useful to study molecules with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conjugated π bonds</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because delocalized π electrons can be excited to higher energy levels when treated with UV light.<br></div><div>- Absorption to higher energy levels causes a change in color, which is useful for detecting changes in chemical reactions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""uv-vis-faq-instrument-design-spectrophotometer-function.png""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;UV&#x20;spectroscopy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[spectroscopy]</span>, electrons with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>small</i></span> <u>energy gaps</u> between their highest-occupied orbital (HOMO) &amp; lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>easier</i></span> to excite."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV spectroscopy</b></span>, electrons with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>small</i></span> <u>energy gaps</u> between their highest-occupied orbital (HOMO) &amp; lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>easier</i></span> to excite.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- These absorb longer wavelengths (lower frequencies) of light...meaning the light they absorb is lower in energy.</div><div>- UV spectroscopy is most useful to study <u>conjugated π bonds</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""uv-vis-faq-instrument-design-spectrophotometer-function.png""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV spectroscopy</b></span>, electrons with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;small&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[small/large]</span> <u>energy gaps</u> between their highest-occupied orbital (HOMO) &amp; lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>easier</i></span> to excite."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV spectroscopy</b></span>, electrons with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>small</i></span> <u>energy gaps</u> between their highest-occupied orbital (HOMO) &amp; lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>easier</i></span> to excite.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- These absorb longer wavelengths (lower frequencies) of light...meaning the light they absorb is lower in energy.</div><div>- UV spectroscopy is most useful to study <u>conjugated π bonds</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""uv-vis-faq-instrument-design-spectrophotometer-function.png""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV spectroscopy</b></span>, electrons with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>small</i></span> <u>energy gaps</u> between their highest-occupied orbital (HOMO) &amp; lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;easier&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[easier/harder]</span> to excite."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>UV spectroscopy</b></span>, electrons with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>small</i></span> <u>energy gaps</u> between their highest-occupied orbital (HOMO) &amp; lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>easier</i></span> to excite.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- These absorb longer wavelengths (lower frequencies) of light...meaning the light they absorb is lower in energy.</div><div>- UV spectroscopy is most useful to study <u>conjugated π bonds</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""uv-vis-faq-instrument-design-spectrophotometer-function.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;NMR&#x20;spectroscopy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[spectroscopy]</span> uses magnetic fields to orient the nuclear spins of certain protons in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">same (α)</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">opposite (β)</span> direction - which get <i>excited </i>by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>radiofrequency irradiations</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>NMR spectroscopy</b></span> uses magnetic fields to orient the nuclear spins of certain protons in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">same (α)</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">opposite (β)</span> direction - which get <i>excited </i>by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>radiofrequency irradiations</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>α-state</b>: aligned in the same direction - giving it lower energy.</div><div>- <b>β-state</b>: aligned in the opposite direction - giving it higher enegry (like potential energy).</div><div>- When you excite the α-state to the β-state, you can tell the structure of a molecule based on the frequency associated with its transition.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""b73225af35b0d35a7d5f875ed53f9cd8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""f1597ae8c5f72b0e29b6dde0ad734223.png""><br></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>NMR spectroscopy</b></span> uses magnetic fields to orient the nuclear spins of certain protons in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""same&#x20;&#x28;α&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""opposite&#x20;&#x28;β&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> direction - which get <i>excited </i>by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>radiofrequency irradiations</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>NMR spectroscopy</b></span> uses magnetic fields to orient the nuclear spins of certain protons in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">same (α)</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">opposite (β)</span> direction - which get <i>excited </i>by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>radiofrequency irradiations</u></span>.
<hr>

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>NMR spectroscopy</b></span> uses magnetic fields to orient the nuclear spins of certain protons in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">same (α)</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">opposite (β)</span> direction - which get <i>excited </i>by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>radiofrequency irradiations</u></span>.

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Represents how close (downfield) or how far (upfield) from electronegative atoms the protons are.</div><div>- Shielding/deshielding refers to other electronegative atoms pulling their electron densities away (so they experience greater forces from the magnetic field).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f1597ae8c5f72b0e29b6dde0ad734223.png""><br></div></div>"
"On an <b>NMR spectra</b>, further to the <i>left </i>on the x-axis is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""down"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>field."	"On an <b>NMR spectra</b>, further to the <i>left </i>on the x-axis is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">down</span>field.

"In NMR spectroscopy, compounds located further <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">down</span>stream are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>deshielded</b></span> due to being <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>closer</i></span> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>electronegative atoms</u></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>a</sub> &amp; H<sub>b</sub> are close enough to the point where they each alter the magnetic field experienced by the other... splitting occurs.</div><div>- n+1 rule determines how many it splits into. For H<sub>b</sub>, there's 1 proton right nearby (n=1), so n+1 means H<sub>b</sub> splits into a <b><u>doublet</u>&nbsp;</b>(1+1=2).</div><div><br></div><div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"<div>In the <b>n+1 rule</b> in NMR spectroscopy, <b>n</b> represents the number of&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""other&#x20;&lt;i&gt;non&#x2D;equivalent&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;protons"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> on a directly adjacent carbon to the proton of focus to determine its <u>splitting</u>.</div>"	"<div>In the <b>n+1 rule</b> in NMR spectroscopy, <b>n</b> represents the number of&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">other <i>non-equivalent&nbsp;</i>protons</span> on a directly adjacent carbon to the proton of focus to determine its <u>splitting</u>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>a</sub> &amp; H<sub>b</sub> are close enough to the point where they each alter the magnetic field experienced by the other... splitting occurs.</div><div>- n+1 rule determines how many it splits into. For H<sub>b</sub>, there's 1 proton right nearby (n=1), so n+1 means H<sub>b</sub> splits into a <u style=""font-weight: bold;"">doublet</u> (1+1=2).</div><div><br></div><div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>n+1 rule</b></span>&nbsp;is <i>only</i> applicable for protons on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>directly adjacent</u></span> carbons, and they <i style="""">cannot</i> be attached to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""oxygen"" data-ordinal=""1"">[element]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""nitrogen"" data-ordinal=""1"">[element]</span>."	"In NMR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>n+1 rule</b></span>&nbsp;is <i>only</i> applicable for protons on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>directly adjacent</u></span> carbons, and they <i style="""">cannot</i> be attached to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">oxygen</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">nitrogen</span>.

"In NMR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;n&#x2B;1&#x20;rule&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is <i>only</i> applicable for protons on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>directly adjacent</u></span> carbons, and they <i style="""">cannot</i> be attached to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">oxygen</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">nitrogen</span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>a</sub> &amp; H<sub>b</sub> are close enough to the point where they each alter the magnetic field experienced by the other... splitting occurs.</div><div>- n+1 rule determines how many it splits into. For H<sub>b</sub>, there's 1 proton right nearby (n=1), so n+1 means H<sub>b</sub> splits into a <u style=""font-weight: bold;"">doublet</u> (1+1=2).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, the <i>magnitude </i>of <u>splitting</u> (doublet, triplet, etc.) is called the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""coupling&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;J&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[constant]</span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>a</sub> &amp; H<sub>b</sub> are close enough to the point where they each alter the magnetic field experienced by the other... splitting occurs.</div><div>- n+1 rule determines how many it splits into. For H<sub>b</sub>, there's 1 proton right nearby (n=1), so n+1 means H<sub>b</sub> splits into a <u style=""font-weight: bold;"">doublet</u> (1+1=2).</div><div><br></div><div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, <u>2 protons</u> connected to the <i>same </i>carbon are always in <b>chemically </b><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;&lt;b&gt;identical&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> environments - meaning they're <u>equivalent</u>."	"In NMR spectroscopy, <u>2 protons</u> connected to the <i>same </i>carbon are always in <b>chemically </b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i><b>identical</b></i></span> environments - meaning they're <u>equivalent</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because they carbon they're attached to can freely rotate around - exposing them both to the same things.</div><div>- Using the n+1 rule, the 2 H<sub>b</sub> protons are considered equivalent, meaning both H<sub>b</sub>'s will present as <b>doublets </b>(since H<sub>a</sub> is the only one that counts as an 'n')... n+1 → 1+1=2.</div><div><br></div><div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, peaks with <i>more </i>than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">4</span> <u>splits</u> are called <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;multiplets&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In NMR spectroscopy, peaks with <i>more </i>than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">4</span> <u>splits</u> are called <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>multiplets</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Using the n+1 rule, the 2 H<sub>b</sub> protons are considered equivalent, meaning both H<sub>b</sub>'s will present as <b>doublets </b>(since H<sub>a</sub> is the only one that counts as an 'n')... n+1 → 1+1=2.<br></div><div><br></div><div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, peaks with <i>more </i>than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""4"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span> <u>splits</u> are called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>multiplets</b></span>."	"In NMR spectroscopy, peaks with <i>more </i>than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">4</span> <u>splits</u> are called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>multiplets</b></span>.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Extraction&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[extract./wash.]</span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">compound of interest</span> from an <u>impure layer</u>, whereas <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;washing&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[extract./wash.]</span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">impurities</span> from the <u>pure layer</u>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Extraction</b></span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">compound of interest</span> from an <u>impure layer</u>, whereas <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>washing</b></span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">impurities</span> from the <u>pure layer</u>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""328af42aab30ec5747fcb889872c258d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""6d39f1cc05221fb84c515d34b8c86ea4.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Extraction</b></span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""compound&#x20;of&#x20;interest"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> from an <u>impure layer</u>, whereas <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>washing</b></span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""impurities"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> from the <u>pure layer</u>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Extraction</b></span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">compound of interest</span> from an <u>impure layer</u>, whereas <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>washing</b></span> is when you add a solute to <i>pull out</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">impurities</span> from the <u>pure layer</u>.

<div><img src=""328af42aab30ec5747fcb889872c258d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""6d39f1cc05221fb84c515d34b8c86ea4.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Filtration&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[technique]</span> allows you to <i>separate</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">solids</span> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquids</span> by placing your mixture on a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>filter paper</u></span> &amp; allowing the solvent to <i>drip </i>through."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Filtration</b></span> allows you to <i>separate</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">solids</span> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquids</span> by placing your mixture on a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>filter paper</u></span> &amp; allowing the solvent to <i>drip </i>through.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This leaves just the solid (residue) behind, while the solvent has collected in the flask.</div><div><br></div><img src=""tmp30127_thumb1.png""></div>"

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Gravity&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[gravity/vacuum]</span> <b>filtration</b> is more commonly used when your <u>compound of interest</u> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">filtrate</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Gravity</b></span> <b>filtration</b> is more commonly used when your <u>compound of interest</u> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">filtrate</span>.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Simple</b></span> <b>distillation </b>should be used to separate <u>two liquids</u> from each other when there's a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>large</i></span> <i>difference </i>in their <u>boiling points</u> (bigger than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">25</span>°C).

<hr>

"In the simple distillation apparatus, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;receiving&#x20;flask&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> collects the purified distillate at the end."	"In the simple distillation apparatus, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>receiving flask</b></span> collects the purified distillate at the end.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-c567c7bb7fc2fa55d0908099f5c8a0bede6a3173.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In simple distillation, <u>boiling chips</u> or <u>stir bars</u> are added to the distilling flask to break up the liquid's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""surface&#x20;tension"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to <i>prevent&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""superheating"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In simple distillation, <u>boiling chips</u> or <u>stir bars</u> are added to the distilling flask to break up the liquid's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">surface tension</span> to <i>prevent&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">superheating</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Superheating</b>: when a liquid is heated above its boiling point but fails to vaporize due to excess tension at the liquid surface (from both surface tension &amp; atmospheric pressure ""weighing down"").</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-c567c7bb7fc2fa55d0908099f5c8a0bede6a3173.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In distillation, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;superheating&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is when the liquid is heated&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>above</i>&nbsp;its boiling point</span> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>fails&nbsp;</i>to&nbsp;</u><u>vaporize</u></span> due to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">excessive surface tension</span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lack</i> of nucleation sites</span>."	"In distillation, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>superheating</b></span> is when the liquid is heated&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>above</i>&nbsp;its boiling point</span> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>fails&nbsp;</i>to&nbsp;</u><u>vaporize</u></span> due to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">excessive surface tension</span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lack</i> of nucleation sites</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Adding <b>boiling chips</b> or <b>stir bars </b>helps to break up this surface tension - allowing the desired liquid to vaporize (as intended).</div><div>- <b>Nucleation sites</b> are the locations within the liquid where bubbles first form. Boiling chips provide increased surface area for bubbles to form - ↑nucleation sites, which also reduces the surface tension.</div><div>- Superheating (due to excessive surface tension) makes it harder for bubbles to form.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9a6ad166026775134d89154f5a94d22a3e1af29f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In distillation, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>superheating</b></span> is when the liquid is heated&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;above&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;its&#x20;boiling&#x20;point"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> but <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;fails&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;to&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;u&gt;vaporize&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> due to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">excessive surface tension</span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lack</i> of nucleation sites</span>."	"In distillation, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>superheating</b></span> is when the liquid is heated&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>above</i>&nbsp;its boiling point</span> but <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>fails&nbsp;</i>to&nbsp;</u><u>vaporize</u></span> due to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">excessive surface tension</span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lack</i> of nucleation sites</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Adding <b>boiling chips</b> or <b>stir bars </b>helps to break up this surface tension - allowing the desired liquid to vaporize (as intended).</div><div>- <b>Nucleation sites</b> are the locations within the liquid where bubbles first form. Boiling chips provide increased surface area for bubbles to form - ↑nucleation sites, which also reduces the surface tension.</div><div>- Superheating (due to excessive surface tension) makes it harder for bubbles to form.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9a6ad166026775134d89154f5a94d22a3e1af29f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In distillation, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>superheating</b></span> is when the liquid is heated&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>above</i>&nbsp;its boiling point</span> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>fails&nbsp;</i>to&nbsp;</u><u>vaporize</u></span> due to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""excessive&#x20;surface&#x20;tension"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lack&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;nucleation&#x20;sites"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"In distillation, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>superheating</b></span> is when the liquid is heated&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>above</i>&nbsp;its boiling point</span> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>fails&nbsp;</i>to&nbsp;</u><u>vaporize</u></span> due to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">excessive surface tension</span> &amp; a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lack</i> of nucleation sites</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Adding <b>boiling chips</b> or <b>stir bars </b>helps to break up this surface tension - allowing the desired liquid to vaporize (as intended).</div><div>- <b>Nucleation sites</b> are the locations within the liquid where bubbles first form. Boiling chips provide increased surface area for bubbles to form - ↑nucleation sites, which also reduces the surface tension.</div><div>- Superheating (due to excessive surface tension) makes it harder for bubbles to form.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-9a6ad166026775134d89154f5a94d22a3e1af29f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Vacuum</b></span> <b>distillation </b>is used when compounds have boiling points <i>above </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""150"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>°C, since the vacuum <i>reduces </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>atmospheric pressure</u></span> &amp; makes it <i>easier </i>for compounds to boil at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">lower temperatures</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Vacuum</b></span> <b>distillation </b>is used when compounds have boiling points <i>above </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">150</span>°C, since the vacuum <i>reduces </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>atmospheric pressure</u></span> &amp; makes it <i>easier </i>for compounds to boil at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">lower temperatures</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This can make it harder to separate two liquids with somewhat similar boiling points though. Account for this by making the column even longer.</div><div>- Allowing it to boil at a lower temperature means we don't have to worry about applying a temperature so high that the compound gets degraded.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a6321f80975933dbb9eb30dd26793cb8a8fae203.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Vacuum&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <b>distillation </b>is used when compounds have boiling points <i>above </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">150</span>°C, since the vacuum <i>reduces </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>atmospheric pressure</u></span> &amp; makes it <i>easier </i>for compounds to boil at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">lower temperatures</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Vacuum</b></span> <b>distillation </b>is used when compounds have boiling points <i>above </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">150</span>°C, since the vacuum <i>reduces </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>atmospheric pressure</u></span> &amp; makes it <i>easier </i>for compounds to boil at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">lower temperatures</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This can make it harder to separate two liquids with somewhat similar boiling points though. Account for this by making the column even longer.</div><div>- Allowing it to boil at a lower temperature means we don't have to worry about applying a temperature so high that the compound gets degraded.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a6321f80975933dbb9eb30dd26793cb8a8fae203.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Vacuum</b></span> <b>distillation </b>is used when compounds have boiling points <i>above </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">150</span>°C, since the vacuum <i>reduces </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;atmospheric&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> &amp; makes it <i>easier </i>for compounds to boil at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""lower&#x20;temperatures"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Vacuum</b></span> <b>distillation </b>is used when compounds have boiling points <i>above </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">150</span>°C, since the vacuum <i>reduces </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>atmospheric pressure</u></span> &amp; makes it <i>easier </i>for compounds to boil at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">lower temperatures</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This can make it harder to separate two liquids with somewhat similar boiling points though. Account for this by making the column even longer.</div><div>- Allowing it to boil at a lower temperature means we don't have to worry about applying a temperature so high that the compound gets degraded.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a6321f80975933dbb9eb30dd26793cb8a8fae203.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;fractional&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[simple/fract.]</span> <b>distillation</b>, wool is packed inside the distilling column to <i>increase </i>its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>surface area</u></span> - giving the liquid <i>more room</i> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">condense &amp; re-evaporate</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>fractional</b></span> <b>distillation</b>, wool is packed inside the distilling column to <i>increase </i>its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>surface area</u></span> - giving the liquid <i>more room</i> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">condense &amp; re-evaporate</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>After each condensation/evaporation cycle, the distillate gets <u>more and more pure</u> (which is important when their boiling points are so close). Giving it a higher surface area means this can happen more.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-94f08f687b88a4a74a39201c2dccbafc6a2cb1c8.jpg""><br></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>fractional</b></span> <b>distillation</b>, wool is packed inside the distilling column to <i>increase </i>its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;surface&#x20;area&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - giving the liquid <i>more room</i> to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""condense&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;re&#x2D;evaporate"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>fractional</b></span> <b>distillation</b>, wool is packed inside the distilling column to <i>increase </i>its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>surface area</u></span> - giving the liquid <i>more room</i> to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">condense &amp; re-evaporate</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>After each condensation/evaporation cycle, the distillate gets <u>more and more pure</u> (which is important when their boiling points are so close). Giving it a higher surface area means this can happen more.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-94f08f687b88a4a74a39201c2dccbafc6a2cb1c8.jpg""><br></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Thin&#x2D;layer&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[thin/paper]</span> <b>chromatography </b>uses either <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>silica</u></span> or&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alumina</u></span> as the medium for its stationary phase." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Called paper chromatography since the paper is made of cellulose.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c2f41b881f88a53a615c059e213af0f6.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Paper&#x2F;TLC&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[chromatography]</span>, the plate is placed in a chamber with a shallow pool of <u>solvent</u> on the bottom, which <i>creeps up</i> the plate via <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">capillary action</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Paper/TLC</b></span>, the plate is placed in a chamber with a shallow pool of <u>solvent</u> on the bottom, which <i>creeps up</i> the plate via <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">capillary action</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- When the solvent rises up the plate, it carries the spotted samples along with it. Separation occurs based on how well the sample adheres to the silica/alumina or cellulose plate.</div><div>- TLC and paper chromatography are the same in all regards, except TLC uses silica/alumina whereas paper chromatography uses cellulseo (paper).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-044600294a74749cdbb5ecc9e0552085f1a00978.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c2f41b881f88a53a615c059e213af0f6.png""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Under <u>normal conditions</u>...</div><div>- <b>Solvent </b>(mobile) is non-polar; <b>plate </b>(stationary) is polar.</div><div>- When the solvent rises up the plate, it carries the spotted samples along with it. Separation occurs based on how well the sample adheres to the silica/alumina or cellulose plate.</div><div>- TLC and paper chromatography are the same in all regards, except TLC uses silica/alumina whereas paper chromatography uses cellulseo (paper).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c2f41b881f88a53a615c059e213af0f6.png""><br></div></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Under <u>normal conditions</u>...</div><div>- <b>Solvent </b>(mobile) is non-polar; <b>plate </b>(stationary) is polar.</div><div>- When the solvent rises up the plate, it carries the spotted samples along with it. Separation occurs based on how well the sample adheres to the silica/alumina or cellulose plate.</div><div>- TLC and paper chromatography are the same in all regards, except TLC uses silica/alumina whereas paper chromatography uses cellulseo (paper).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c2f41b881f88a53a615c059e213af0f6.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Flash&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>chromatography </b>is a form of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">column</span> chromatography when a<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high&nbsp;</i><u>air pressure</u></span> is used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>force</i>&nbsp;out the solvent through the column</span> quickly."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Flash</b></span> <b>chromatography </b>is a form of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">column</span> chromatography when a<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high&nbsp;</i><u>air pressure</u></span> is used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>force</i>&nbsp;out the solvent through the column</span> quickly.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""0a14e87bac9aced7d8b3efe97c7830d3.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Flash</b></span> <b>chromatography </b>is a form of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""column"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> chromatography when a<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;air&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is used to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;force&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;out&#x20;the&#x20;solvent&#x20;through&#x20;the&#x20;column"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> quickly."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Flash</b></span> <b>chromatography </b>is a form of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">column</span> chromatography when a<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high&nbsp;</i><u>air pressure</u></span> is used to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>force</i>&nbsp;out the solvent through the column</span> quickly.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""0a14e87bac9aced7d8b3efe97c7830d3.png""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;column&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>chromatography</b>, <u>alumina/silica</u> is <i>packed</i> into the column to separate out compounds based on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">polarity</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>column</b></span> <b>chromatography</b>, <u>alumina/silica</u> is <i>packed</i> into the column to separate out compounds based on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">polarity</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Alumina/silica</b>: polar (stationary).</div><div>- <b>Solvent</b>: non-polar (mobile).</div><div><br></div><img src=""0a14e87bac9aced7d8b3efe97c7830d3.png""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>column</b></span> <b>chromatography</b>, <u>alumina/silica</u> is <i>packed</i> into the column to separate out compounds based on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""polarity"" data-ordinal=""2"">[property]</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>column</b></span> <b>chromatography</b>, <u>alumina/silica</u> is <i>packed</i> into the column to separate out compounds based on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">polarity</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Alumina/silica</b>: polar (stationary).</div><div>- <b>Solvent</b>: non-polar (mobile).</div><div><br></div><img src=""0a14e87bac9aced7d8b3efe97c7830d3.png""></div>"
"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;normal&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[normal/reverse]</span><b>-phase</b>&nbsp;chromatography, the <u>stationary</u> phase is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>polar</i></span> &amp; the <u>mobile</u> phase is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-polar</i></span>.</div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>normal</b></span><b>-phase</b>&nbsp;chromatography, the <u>stationary</u> phase is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>polar</i></span> &amp; the <u>mobile</u> phase is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-polar</i></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>Polar</b> compounds elute slower (more interactions with polar stationary phase).<div>- <b>Non-polar</b> compounds elute faster (less interaction with polar stationary phase).</div></div>"
"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>normal</b></span><b>-phase</b>&nbsp;chromatography, the <u>stationary</u> phase is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;polar&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[polar/non-polar]</span> &amp; the <u>mobile</u> phase is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;non&#x2D;polar&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[polar/non-polar]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>normal</b></span><b>-phase</b>&nbsp;chromatography, the <u>stationary</u> phase is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>polar</i></span> &amp; the <u>mobile</u> phase is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-polar</i></span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>Polar</b> compounds elute slower (more interactions with polar stationary phase).<div>- <b>Non-polar</b> compounds elute faster (less interaction with polar stationary phase).</div></div>"

"In <b>gas</b>&nbsp;<b>chromatography</b>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>volatile</i></span> compound of interest is crushed &amp; injected into the machine, where <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""N&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> gas carries it through the column."	"In <b>gas</b>&nbsp;<b>chromatography</b>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>volatile</i></span> compound of interest is crushed &amp; injected into the machine, where <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub></span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">N<sub>2</sub></span> gas carries it through the column.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Separates molecules based on boiling point/volatility. More volatile it is, sooner it'll vaporize and the sooner it'll pass through the entire column.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-1a2d826717f1ff238464cbfa360739a31b4fa6e2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Gas</b></span> <b>chromatography </b>separates molecules based on their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;boiling&#x20;points&#x2F;volatility&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Gas</b></span> <b>chromatography </b>separates molecules based on their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>boiling points/volatility</u></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- More volatile it is, sooner it'll vaporize and the sooner it'll pass through the entire column.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-1a2d826717f1ff238464cbfa360739a31b4fa6e2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <b>gas chromatography</b>, compounds with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[higher/lower]</span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span>, while <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[higher/lower]</span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">last</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <b>gas chromatography</b>, compounds with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span>, while <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">last</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>More volatile it is, the sooner it'll vaporize and the sooner it'll pass through the entire column.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-1a2d826717f1ff238464cbfa360739a31b4fa6e2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <b>gas chromatography</b>, compounds with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""first"" data-ordinal=""2"">[first/last]</span>, while <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""last"" data-ordinal=""2"">[first/last]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <b>gas chromatography</b>, compounds with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span>, while <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <u>boiling points</u> will elute <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">last</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>More volatile it is, the sooner it'll vaporize and the sooner it'll pass through the entire column.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-1a2d826717f1ff238464cbfa360739a31b4fa6e2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Mass&#x20;spectrometry&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> determines the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular weights</u></span> of compounds by fragmenting them into free radicals/cations &amp; comparing their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">mass-to-charge ratios</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mass spectrometry</b></span> determines the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular weights</u></span> of compounds by fragmenting them into free radicals/cations &amp; comparing their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">mass-to-charge ratios</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Only the cation fragments get detected by the mass spectrometer.</div><div><br></div><img src=""fa959e706bc07ba7e88247d1c68fa943.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-aeb8369e7bd0b2140bc8f02b6c9237948d1f34e5.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mass spectrometry</b></span> determines the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;molecular&#x20;weights&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of compounds by fragmenting them into free radicals/cations &amp; comparing their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""mass&#x2D;to&#x2D;charge&#x20;ratios"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mass spectrometry</b></span> determines the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular weights</u></span> of compounds by fragmenting them into free radicals/cations &amp; comparing their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">mass-to-charge ratios</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Only the cation fragments get detected by the mass spectrometer.</div><div><br></div><img src=""fa959e706bc07ba7e88247d1c68fa943.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-aeb8369e7bd0b2140bc8f02b6c9237948d1f34e5.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;HPLC&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>non-volatile</i></span> compounds are injected into a machine and carried in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>liquid</i></span> phase through the column under <u>high pressures</u>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>HPLC</b></span> is when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>non-volatile</i></span> compounds are injected into a machine and carried in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>liquid</i></span> phase through the column under <u>high pressures</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Separates by how well the compound adheres to the liquid (mobile) phase.</div><div>- Very similar to gas phase chromatography, except this uses the liquid phase instead of the gas phase (meaning it doesn't separate by volatility).</div><div><br></div><img src=""High-Performance-Liquid-Chromatography-HPLC.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>HPLC</b></span> is when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>non-volatile</i></span> compounds are injected into a machine and carried in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;liquid&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> phase through the column under <u>high pressures</u>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>HPLC</b></span> is when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>non-volatile</i></span> compounds are injected into a machine and carried in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>liquid</i></span> phase through the column under <u>high pressures</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Separates by how well the compound adheres to the liquid (mobile) phase.</div><div>- Very similar to gas phase chromatography, except this uses the liquid phase instead of the gas phase (meaning it doesn't separate by volatility).</div><div><br></div><img src=""High-Performance-Liquid-Chromatography-HPLC.jpg""></div>"
"In <b>gas</b>&nbsp;<b>chromatography</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;volatile&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[volatile/non-volatile]</span> compound of interest is crushed &amp; injected into the machine, where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">N<sub>2</sub></span> gas carries it through the column."	"In <b>gas</b>&nbsp;<b>chromatography</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>volatile</i></span> compound of interest is crushed &amp; injected into the machine, where <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">N<sub>2</sub></span> gas carries it through the column.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Separates molecules based on boiling point/volatility. More volatile it is, sooner it'll vaporize and the sooner it'll pass through the entire column.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-1a2d826717f1ff238464cbfa360739a31b4fa6e2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>HPLC</b></span> is when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;non&#x2D;volatile&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[volatile/non-volatile]</span> compounds are injected into a machine and carried in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>liquid</i></span> phase through the column under <u>high pressures</u>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>HPLC</b></span> is when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>non-volatile</i></span> compounds are injected into a machine and carried in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>liquid</i></span> phase through the column under <u>high pressures</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Separates by how well the compound adheres to the liquid (mobile) phase.</div><div>- Very similar to gas phase chromatography, except this uses the liquid phase instead of the gas phase (meaning it doesn't separate by volatility).</div><div><br></div><img src=""High-Performance-Liquid-Chromatography-HPLC.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In NMR spectroscopy, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>radiofrequency irradiations</b></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>excite</i>&nbsp;α-state protons to the β-state</span> - allowing us to measure the resulting <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;changes&#x20;in&#x20;&lt;i&gt;frequency&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span><i>&nbsp;</i>associated with its structure.</div>"	"<div>In NMR spectroscopy, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>radiofrequency irradiations</b></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>excite</i>&nbsp;α-state protons to the β-state</span> - allowing us to measure the resulting <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>changes in <i>frequency</i></u></span><i>&nbsp;</i>associated with its structure.</div>

"<b>Esterification</b> reactions are accelerated in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;acidic&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acidic/basic]</span> solutions, because it allows the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carbonyl's oxygen</u></span> to get <i>protonated </i>- making the carbonyl carbon more electrophilic." Here, Br would have the highest priority.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ddcad95900a7cf0c7ceb115ffb27e15ef9787963.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Two different <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;enantiomers&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[enant./diast.]</span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other in solution by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>reacting </i>them with chiral reagents</span>."	"Two different <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>enantiomers</b></span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other in solution by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>reacting </i>them with chiral reagents</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because <b>enantiomers </b>have <u>identical physical properties</u>.</div><div>- <b>Diastereomers </b>can be separated through things like fractional distillation, since they differ slightly in their boiling points (different physical properties).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-de54725dd0b603fc15e8e746df8b29f6e0e3d4c4.jpg""><br></div>"
"Two different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>enantiomers</b></span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other in solution by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;reacting&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;them&#x20;with&#x20;chiral&#x20;reagents"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"Two different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>enantiomers</b></span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other in solution by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>reacting </i>them with chiral reagents</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because <b>enantiomers </b>have <u>identical physical properties</u>.</div><div>- <b>Diastereomers </b>can be separated through things like fractional distillation, since they differ slightly in their boiling points (different physical properties).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-de54725dd0b603fc15e8e746df8b29f6e0e3d4c4.jpg""><br></div>"
"Two different <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;diastereomers&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[enant./diast.]</span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">fractional distillation/extraction/etc.</span>."	"Two different <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>diastereomers</b></span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">fractional distillation/extraction/etc.</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The key point is that diastereomers should be separated from each other by protocols that take advantage of their <u>different physical properties</u>.</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Diastereomers&nbsp;</b>can be separated through things like fractional distillation, since they differ slightly in their boiling points (different physical properties).<br></div><div>- This is because <b>enantiomers </b>have <u>identical physical properties</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Chiral_drugs.gif""></div>"
"Two different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>diastereomers</b></span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""fractional&#x20;distillation&#x2F;extraction&#x2F;etc&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"Two different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>diastereomers</b></span> can <i>best </i>be <u>separated</u> from each other by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">fractional distillation/extraction/etc.</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The key point is that diastereomers should be separated from each other by protocols that take advantage of their <u>different physical properties</u>.</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Diastereomers&nbsp;</b>can be separated through things like fractional distillation, since they differ slightly in their boiling points (different physical properties).<br></div><div>- This is because <b>enantiomers </b>have <u>identical physical properties</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Chiral_drugs.gif""></div>"
"<div>Treating carbonyls with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;R&#x2D;MgBr&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>reduce</i> the carbonyl <i>→&nbsp;</i><u>alcohol</u></span> &amp; add the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>R-group</u></span> to the <i>old</i> carbonyl carbon following a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""acidic"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acidic/basic]</span> workup.</div>"	"<div>Treating carbonyls with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>R-MgBr</b></span> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>reduce</i> the carbonyl <i>→&nbsp;</i><u>alcohol</u></span> &amp; add the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>R-group</u></span> to the <i>old</i> carbonyl carbon following a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">acidic</span> workup.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- R-MgBr compounds are <b>Grignard reagents</b>.</div><div>- Before the acidic workup, the MgBr group attaches to the carbonyl's oxygen.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9711e2593b4d5af06c0b25b7c2da0961.png""></div>"
"<div>Treating carbonyls with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>R-MgBr</b></span> will <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;reduce&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;carbonyl&#x20;&lt;i&gt;→&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;alcohol&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; add the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;R&#x2D;group&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> to the <i>old</i> carbonyl carbon following a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">acidic</span> workup.</div>"	"<div>Treating carbonyls with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>R-MgBr</b></span> will <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>reduce</i> the carbonyl <i>→&nbsp;</i><u>alcohol</u></span> &amp; add the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>R-group</u></span> to the <i>old</i> carbonyl carbon following a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">acidic</span> workup.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- R-MgBr compounds are <b>Grignard reagents</b>.</div><div>- Before the acidic workup, the MgBr group attaches to the carbonyl's oxygen.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9711e2593b4d5af06c0b25b7c2da0961.png""></div>"
"<b>Free radical</b> reactions can be <i>initiated </i>with the use of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""UV&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""peroxides"" data-ordinal=""1"">[compounds]</span>."	"<b>Free radical</b> reactions can be <i>initiated </i>with the use of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">UV light</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">peroxides</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- UV light is needed to overcome the energy needed to do this.</div><div>- Homolytically because each bond shares electrons equally - hence creating the free radical.</div><div><br></div><img src=""11add4c9a1391d1d92337e84c53f9ce1.png""></div>"
"<b>H<sub>2</sub>/Pd</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reducing&#x20;agent"" data-ordinal=""1"">[oxidant/reducing agent]</span>."	"<b>H<sub>2</sub>/Pd</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>weak</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">reducing agent</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Can't reduce carboxylic acids, esters, etc.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c67dc30ad520ffdfb67ddcda4a7df4b6.png""></div>"
"<div>This represents an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;E1&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[E1/E2]</span> reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""d13730a340007ac53ed1379ec5a591b7.png"">"	"<div>This represents an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>E1</b></span> reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""d13730a340007ac53ed1379ec5a591b7.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>E1</b> (similar to SN1): prefers 3°, weak bases, and protic solvents.<div>- <b>E2</b> (similar to SN2): prefers 1°, strong bases, and aprotic solvent.</div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;S&lt;sub&gt;N&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S<sub>N</sub>1/S<sub>N</sub>2]</span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">3°</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> bases, and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">protic</span> solvents.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>1</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">3°</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> bases, and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">protic</span> solvents.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>E1</b> (similar to SN1): prefers 3°, weak bases, and protic solvents.<div>- <b>E2</b> (similar to SN2): prefers 1°, strong bases, and aprotic solvent.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d13730a340007ac53ed1379ec5a591b7.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>1</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""3&#xB0;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[1°/2°/3°]</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weak/strong]</span> bases, and <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""protic"" data-ordinal=""2"">[protic/aprotic]</span> solvents.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>1</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">3°</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> bases, and <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">protic</span> solvents.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>E1</b> (similar to SN1): prefers 3°, weak bases, and protic solvents.<div>- <b>E2</b> (similar to SN2): prefers 1°, strong bases, and aprotic solvent.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d13730a340007ac53ed1379ec5a591b7.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;S&lt;sub&gt;N&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S<sub>N</sub>1/S<sub>N</sub>2]</span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1°</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> bases, and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">aprotic</span> solvents.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>2</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1°</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> bases, and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">aprotic</span> solvents.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>E1</b> (similar to SN1): prefers 3°, weak bases, and protic solvents.<div>- <b>E2</b> (similar to SN2): prefers 1°, strong bases, and aprotic solvent.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""b4a365257a84789aeff2b06b6a281291.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>2</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#xB0;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[1°/2°/3°]</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weak/strong]</span> bases, and <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""aprotic"" data-ordinal=""2"">[protic/aprotic]</span> solvents.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>2</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">1°</span> substrates, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> bases, and <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">aprotic</span> solvents.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>E1</b> (similar to SN1): prefers 3°, weak bases, and protic solvents.<div>- <b>E2</b> (similar to SN2): prefers 1°, strong bases, and aprotic solvent.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""b4a365257a84789aeff2b06b6a281291.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;S&lt;sub&gt;N&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;E1&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>protic</u></span> solvents since it aids in carbocation stability."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>1</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>E1</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>protic</u></span> solvents since it aids in carbocation stability.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Elimination reactions (similar to substitution)</u>...</div>- <b>E1</b> (similar to SN1): prefers 3°, weak bases, and protic solvents.<div>- <b>E2</b> (similar to SN2): prefers 1°, strong bases, and aprotic solvent.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>1</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>E1</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;protic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[protic/aprotic]</span> solvents since it aids in carbocation stability."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>S<sub>N</sub>1</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>E1</b></span> reactions prefer <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>protic</u></span> solvents since it aids in carbocation stability.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Elimination reactions (similar to substitution)</u>...</div>- <b>E1</b> (similar to SN1): prefers 3°, weak bases, and protic solvents.<div>- <b>E2</b> (similar to SN2): prefers 1°, strong bases, and aprotic solvent.</div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Williamson&#x20;ether"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;synthesis&nbsp;uses <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alkyl-halides</u> (CH<sub>3</sub>I)</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>strong </i>bases (NaH)</u></span> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ethers</b></span> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">alcohols</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Williamson ether</span>&nbsp;synthesis&nbsp;uses <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alkyl-halides</u> (CH<sub>3</sub>I)</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>strong </i>bases (NaH)</u></span> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ethers</b></span> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">alcohols</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- NaH deprotonates the alcohol (making it a nucleophile) → MeI gets nucleophilically attacked by the deprotonated alcohol.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""03fcca68e096fd3690f228639cb5d45c.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Williamson ether</span>&nbsp;synthesis&nbsp;uses <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;alkyl&#x2D;halides&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;CH&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;I&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;strong&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;bases&#x20;&#x28;NaH&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ethers</b></span> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">alcohols</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Williamson ether</span>&nbsp;synthesis&nbsp;uses <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alkyl-halides</u> (CH<sub>3</sub>I)</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>strong </i>bases (NaH)</u></span> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ethers</b></span> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">alcohols</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- NaH deprotonates the alcohol (making it a nucleophile) → MeI gets nucleophilically attacked by the deprotonated alcohol.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""03fcca68e096fd3690f228639cb5d45c.png""></div>"
"<div><b>CH<sub>3</sub>-I/NaH</b> are <i>example </i>reagents used to prepare <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ethers"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""alcohols"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div><b>CH<sub>3</sub>-I/NaH</b> are <i>example </i>reagents used to prepare <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ethers</span> from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">alcohols</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Williamson ether synthesis</b></div><div>- NaH deprotonates the alcohol (making it a stronger nucleophile) → CH<sub>3</sub> on CH<sub>3</sub>-I gets nucleophilically attacked by the deprotonated alcohol, since the CH<sub>3</sub> has a very strong partial positive when bound to iodine.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""03fcca68e096fd3690f228639cb5d45c.png""></div>"
"<div>In the <b>Williamson ether synthesis</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""strong&#x20;base&#x20;&#x28;NaH&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reagent]</span> <i>deprotonates</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;alcohol&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to make it <i>more nucleophilic</i>&nbsp;to attack the alkyl-halide (MeI).</div>"	"<div>In the <b>Williamson ether synthesis</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">strong base (NaH)</span> <i>deprotonates</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohol</u></span> to make it <i>more nucleophilic</i>&nbsp;to attack the alkyl-halide (MeI).</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Williamson ether synthesis</b></div><div>- NaH deprotonates the alcohol (making it a nucleophile) → MeI gets nucleophilically attacked by the deprotonated alcohol.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""03fcca68e096fd3690f228639cb5d45c.png""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Ethers&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> can be <i>cleaved</i> into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohols</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alkyl halides</u></span> when treated with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">strong acid</span> under a high temperature.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ethers</b></span> can be <i>cleaved</i> into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohols</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alkyl halides</u></span> when treated with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">strong acid</span> under a high temperature.</div>

"<u>Difference</u> between <b>deamination </b>reactions &amp; <b>deamidation </b>reactions...<div><br></div><div>Deamination: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amino&#x20;group&#x27;s&#x20;NH&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;is&#x20;removed&#x20;to&#x20;produce&#x20;ammonia&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>Deamidation: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amide&#x27;s&#x20;NH&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;is&#x20;removed&#x20;to&#x20;produce&#x20;ammonia&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<u>Difference</u> between <b>deamination </b>reactions &amp; <b>deamidation </b>reactions...<div><br></div><div>Deamination: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">amino group's NH<sub>3</sub> is removed to produce ammonia.</span></div><div>Deamidation: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">amide's NH<sub>3</sub> is removed to produce ammonia.</span></div>
