<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""4e13551e4c55b93a2ab4d41ad71b1e21.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c52e10994e4bfea8b2ae9e6433e93aad.png""></div></div>"
"<u>Difference</u> between <b>deamination </b>reactions &amp; <b>deamidation </b>reactions...<div><br></div><div>Deamidation: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amide&#x27;s&#x20;NH&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;is&#x20;removed&#x20;to&#x20;produce&#x20;ammonia&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>Deamination: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amino&#x20;group&#x27;s&#x20;NH&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;is&#x20;removed&#x20;to&#x20;produce&#x20;ammonia&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<u>Difference</u> between <b>deamination </b>reactions &amp; <b>deamidation </b>reactions...<div><br></div><div>Deamidation: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">amide's NH<sub>3</sub> is removed to produce ammonia.</span></div><div>Deamination: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">amino group's NH<sub>3</sub> is removed to produce ammonia.</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Released ammonia then undergoes the urea cycle.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""4e13551e4c55b93a2ab4d41ad71b1e21.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c52e10994e4bfea8b2ae9e6433e93aad.png""></div></div>"
"<div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""β"" data-ordinal=""1"">[α/β/γ]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""γ"" data-ordinal=""1"">[α/β/γ]</span> phosphates on <u>nucleoside triphosphates</u> are <i>released</i> as <b>pyrophosphate</b>.</div>"	"<div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">β</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">γ</span> phosphates on <u>nucleoside triphosphates</u> are <i>released</i> as <b>pyrophosphate</b>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- ATP, GTP, adenosine, cytosine, etc.</div><div>- α phosphate is nucleophilically attacked by the other molecule -&nbsp;releasing the β &amp; γ phosphates as pyrophosphate.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d02816e3b400802d1583fce46f9e9020a94358b8.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Peptide bond formation</b> results in the <i>loss</i> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""water"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> molecule, since it's a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>dehydration</u></span> reaction." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Bad leaving groups (like alcohols) can be converted into <b>tosylate</b> <b>groups</b> to allow them to act as <u>good leaving groups</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ee113f8e70a2d716f160ca442e2673701234f543.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Adding <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Br&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x2F;H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reagents]</span> to an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""alkene"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> adds the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>OH</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>more</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Br</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position."	"Adding <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Br<sub>2</sub>/H<sub>2</sub>O</b></span> to an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkene</span> adds the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>OH</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>more</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Br</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Have an <u>anti</u> stereochemistry (anti-periplanar).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6fae1457f88544b1e2e39fa292cf23de8b28d91f.jpg""></div>"
"Adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Br<sub>2</sub>/H<sub>2</sub>O</b></span> to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkene</span> adds the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;OH&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>more</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Br&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position."	"Adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Br<sub>2</sub>/H<sub>2</sub>O</b></span> to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkene</span> adds the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>OH</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>more</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Br</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Have an <u>anti</u> stereochemistry (anti-periplanar).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6fae1457f88544b1e2e39fa292cf23de8b28d91f.jpg""></div>"
"Adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Br<sub>2</sub>/H<sub>2</sub>O</b></span> to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkene</span> adds the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>OH</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[less/more]</span>&nbsp;substituted position &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Br</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[less/more]</span>&nbsp;substituted position."	"Adding <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Br<sub>2</sub>/H<sub>2</sub>O</b></span> to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkene</span> adds the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>OH</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>more</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Br</u></span> to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;substituted position.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Have an <u>anti</u> stereochemistry (anti-periplanar).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6fae1457f88544b1e2e39fa292cf23de8b28d91f.jpg""></div>"
"<div>The addition of <b>Br</b> and <b>OH</b> to an <u>alkene</u> (bromination) has <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""anti"" data-ordinal=""1"">[syn/anti]</span> stereospecificity.<br></div>"	"<div>The addition of <b>Br</b> and <b>OH</b> to an <u>alkene</u> (bromination) has <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">anti</span> stereospecificity.<br></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>One's a <u>wedge</u> while one's a <u>dash</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-6fae1457f88544b1e2e39fa292cf23de8b28d91f.jpg""></div>"
"In the <u>Gabriel synthesis</u>, the substitution reaction between <b>potassium phthalimide</b> &amp; <b>diethyl bromomalonate</b> is an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""S&lt;sub&gt;N&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;2"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S<sub>N</sub>1/S<sub>N</sub>2]</span> reaction." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-b14c41bbc612e3e7821491ef25ded7d37c819d49.jpg""></div>"
"In designating S/R configuration of <b>amino acids </b>(excluding cysteine), the priority goes as follows...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""NH&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> (highest priority)</div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""COOH"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""R&#x2D;group"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>4. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> (lowest priority)</div>"	"In designating S/R configuration of <b>amino acids </b>(excluding cysteine), the priority goes as follows...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">NH<sub>2</sub></span> (highest priority)</div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">COOH</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">R-group</span></div><div>4. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">H</span> (lowest priority)</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Cysteine is excluded because it's the only amino acid with an R-group higher in priority than its COOH group.</div><div>- Only moving one atom out, nitrogen has the highest atomic number (hence why it's the highest priority).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5dd3fe2a371feebd934d860f0a19219cf97b26b2.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-af6f4123f0a4b9f78c2bf4c60a522d5ab1122c82.jpg""><br></div></div>"

</div>"
"<div><b>Geometric isomers</b>&nbsp;(cis/trans isomers)&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[the same/different]</span> <u>physical properties</u>.<br></div>"	"<div><b>Geometric isomers</b>&nbsp;(cis/trans isomers)&nbsp;have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <u>physical properties</u>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Geometric isomers can be separated from each other using gas chromatography, since they have different boiling points.<div>- <b>Trans</b> <b>isomers</b> have <u>higher boiling points</u> due to their increased stackability, for example.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-88356fd07a14c210f3c4af6eb54cd5fad54187c9.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Esters</b></span> are formed when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> react with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;acidic&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[acidic/basic]</span> conditions.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Esters</b></span> are formed when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> react with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>alcohols</u></span> in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>acidic</i></span> conditions.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Happens fastest with primary alcohols</div><div>- Acidic solutions because it protonates the carbonyl's oxygen atom into OH - making the carbonyl even more electrophilic (making it easier for the alcohol to attack).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-c37f315f0df8112a7f5c651e7d33448b7a3b0aa0.jpg""><br></div>"
"In order for a molecule to be considered a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>aromatic ring</b></span>, 4 criteria must be met:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Conjugated&#x20;π&#x2D;bonds"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Delocalized&#x20;p&#x2D;orbitals"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in each atom.</div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Planar"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> geometry.</div><div>4. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""4n&#x2B;2"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> π eletrons.</div>"	"In order for a molecule to be considered a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>aromatic ring</b></span>, 4 criteria must be met:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Conjugated π-bonds</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Delocalized p-orbitals</span> in each atom.</div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Planar</span> geometry.</div><div>4. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">4n+2</span> π eletrons.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>4n+2 π electrons is also called&nbsp;<b>Hückel's rule</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e3bb91d54833c60f2c1a803b1f47673b2dfdc1e0.jpg""></div>"
"In order for a molecule to be considered a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;aromatic&#x20;ring&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, 4 criteria must be met:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Conjugated π-bonds</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Delocalized p-orbitals</span> in each atom.</div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Planar</span> geometry.</div><div>4. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">4n+2</span> π eletrons.</div>"	"In order for a molecule to be considered a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>aromatic ring</b></span>, 4 criteria must be met:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Conjugated π-bonds</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Delocalized p-orbitals</span> in each atom.</div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Planar</span> geometry.</div><div>4. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">4n+2</span> π eletrons.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>4n+2 π electrons is also called&nbsp;<b>Hückel's rule</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e3bb91d54833c60f2c1a803b1f47673b2dfdc1e0.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>LDA</b></span>&nbsp;is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;large&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[small/large]</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""base"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acid/base]</span> that forms a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>enolate</u></span> by abstracting a proton from the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""kinetic"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/thermodynamic]</span> α-carbon."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>LDA</b></span>&nbsp;is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>large</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">base</span> that forms a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>enolate</u></span> by abstracting a proton from the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">kinetic</span> α-carbon.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Small bases favor the thermodynamic product.</div><div>- Large bases favor the kinetic product.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3f790090d8d1d6d85fe0ea34b9b71924c5b07544.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Decarboxylation</b> <b>reactions</b> can <i>only</i> occur on molecules with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""β&#x2D;dicarbonyl"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> groups."	"<b>Decarboxylation</b> <b>reactions</b> can <i>only</i> occur on molecules with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">β-dicarbonyl</span> groups.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Decarboxylation canonly happen on compounds with 2 carbonyl groups next to each other (β carbons apart).</div><div>- In metabolic pathways (like the TCA), decarboxylation only occurs on intermediates with β-dicarbonyl groups.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b172aea7df93e9ac89568c3f01e42ca0f85cf9d8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8dfd6021b91d3db5ee13188874e7d6dc.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Diketones&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> that result in the <i>formation</i> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cyclic ring</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Diketones</u></span>&nbsp;undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> that result in the <i>formation</i> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cyclic ring</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-41e04a35814f647a3ad9892d2010d8ef961532c9.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Diketones</u></span>&nbsp;undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> that result in the <i>formation</i> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cyclic&#x20;ring"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Diketones</u></span>&nbsp;undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> that result in the <i>formation</i> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">cyclic ring</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-41e04a35814f647a3ad9892d2010d8ef961532c9.jpg""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x2C;4"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#,#]</span>-diketones undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">5</span>-membered rings.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1,4</span>-diketones undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> to form <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">5</span>-membered rings.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The ring will always have one more ""member"" than the number of carbons connecting them.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-41e04a35814f647a3ad9892d2010d8ef961532c9.jpg""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1,4</span>-diketones undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> to form <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""5"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span>-membered rings.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1,4</span>-diketones undergo <b>aldol condensations</b> to form <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">5</span>-membered rings.<br></div>

<div>The ring will always have one more ""member"" than the number of carbons connecting them.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-41e04a35814f647a3ad9892d2010d8ef961532c9.jpg""></div>"

"In <u>mass spectrometers</u>, <i>ionized</i> molecules are accelerated towards a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""magnetic&#x20;field"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> and <i>deflected</i> away from it based on its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""molecular&#x20;weight"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In <u>mass spectrometers</u>, <i>ionized</i> molecules are accelerated towards a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">magnetic field</span> and <i>deflected</i> away from it based on its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">molecular weight</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is how mass spectrometry is able to detect a compound's molecular weight.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d2ea07ef30b47d06dcd5f9c5dac3011dc4aeddec.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-aeb8369e7bd0b2140bc8f02b6c9237948d1f34e5.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Litmus papers</b> turn <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;red&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[red/blue]</span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>acidic</u></span> solutions." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Acidic conditions → carboxylic acid &amp; alcohol.</div><div>- Basic conditions → carboxylate ion &amp; alcohol.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-75a4bbd29f4621521c7dd45ddbdabf90d9cba57f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fluorophores</b></span> are <u>conjugated π systems</u> that emit photons of light with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>less</i></span> energy &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>larger</i></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">wavelengths</span> than what they absorbed, due to a loss of energy as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""heat"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fluorophores</b></span> are <u>conjugated π systems</u> that emit photons of light with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>less</i></span> energy &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>larger</i></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">wavelengths</span> than what they absorbed, due to a loss of energy as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">heat</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Notice how it <u>absorbs</u> at 340 nm but <u>emits</u> at 541 nm.</div><div>- <b>Fluorophores</b> are essentially just compounds capable of <u>fluorescing</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-eb6a4b2011dd00137cd512d1da7ba3650e731119.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-abd45222af1258bd0a42f962632ef0f02c957ff3.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Williamson ether</span>&nbsp;synthesis&nbsp;uses <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alkyl-halides</u> (CH<sub>3</sub>I)</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>strong </i>bases (NaH)</u></span> to form <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ethers&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[groups]</span> from <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""alcohols"" data-ordinal=""3"">[groups]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Williamson ether</span>&nbsp;synthesis&nbsp;uses <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alkyl-halides</u> (CH<sub>3</sub>I)</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>strong </i>bases (NaH)</u></span> to form <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ethers</b></span> from <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">alcohols</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- NaH deprotonates the alcohol (making it a nucleophile) → MeI gets nucleophilically attacked by the deprotonated alcohol.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""03fcca68e096fd3690f228639cb5d45c.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Hydrogen bonding</u></span> affects the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>boiling point</b></span>&nbsp;of a compound <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> than its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>stackability</u> (Van der Waal forces)</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Hydrogen bonding</u></span> affects the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>boiling point</b></span>&nbsp;of a compound <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> than its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>stackability</u> (Van der Waal forces)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Hydrogen bonding is a significantly stronger intermolecular force, and the boiling point of a compound is directly related to the strength of its intermolecular forces.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-94a1b5e343bc46a0d27a917b349e486961e5527a.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Hydrogen&#x20;bonding&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[hydrogen bond/stacking]</span> affects the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>boiling point</b></span>&nbsp;of a compound <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> than its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;stackability&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;Van&#x20;der&#x20;Waal&#x20;forces&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[hydrogen bond/stacking]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Hydrogen bonding</u></span> affects the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>boiling point</b></span>&nbsp;of a compound <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> than its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>stackability</u> (Van der Waal forces)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"In <b>NMR spectroscopy</b>, molecules with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more]</span>&nbsp;<u>hydrogen bonding</u> are found further <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">up</span>field."	"In <b>NMR spectroscopy</b>, molecules with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;<u>hydrogen bonding</u> are found further <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">up</span>field.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-ae09fa087cbdd1afda63a7eb16ab256a65b31d46.jpg""></div>"
"<b>HPLC</b> separates <i>non-volatile</i> liquids from each other on the basis of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""polarity"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span>."	"<b>HPLC</b> separates <i>non-volatile</i> liquids from each other on the basis of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">polarity</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The stronger they adhere to the stationary phase, the higher their retention times.</div><div>- The better they adhere to the mobile phase, the shorter their retention times.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-381d69cd1a65e9968a3e81c7ef28d40a91db82b0.jpg""></div>"
"<b>HPLC</b> uses <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""silica&#x20;gel"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> as its <u>stationary</u> phase."	"<b>HPLC</b> uses <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">silica gel</span> as its <u>stationary</u> phase.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>HPLC</b> injects non-volatile liquids into the machine to separate them on the basis of polarity.</div><div><br></div><div>- The stronger they adhere to the stationary phase, the higher their retention times.</div><div>- The better they adhere to the mobile phase, the shorter their retention times.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-381d69cd1a65e9968a3e81c7ef28d40a91db82b0.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In <b>HPLC</b>, molecules that adhere <i>more</i> to the stationary phase have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;longer&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[shorter/longer]</span> <u>retention times</u>.<br></div>"	"<div>In <b>HPLC</b>, molecules that adhere <i>more</i> to the stationary phase have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>longer</i></span> <u>retention times</u>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>HPLC</b>&nbsp;injects non-volatile liquids into the machine to separate them on the basis of polarity.</div><div>- The same principle of retention timing applies to gas chromatography, except HPLC uses non-volatile liquids.</div><div><br></div><div>- The stronger they adhere to the stationary phase, the higher their retention times.</div><div>- The better they adhere to the mobile phase, the shorter their retention times.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-381d69cd1a65e9968a3e81c7ef28d40a91db82b0.jpg""><div></div></div>"
"<div>In <b>HPLC</b>, molecules that adhere <i>more</i> to the mobile phase have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;shorter&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[shorter/longer]</span> <u>retention times</u>.<br></div>"	"<div>In <b>HPLC</b>, molecules that adhere <i>more</i> to the mobile phase have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>shorter</i></span> <u>retention times</u>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>HPLC</b> injects non-volatile liquids into the machine to separate them on the basis of polarity.</div><div>- The same principle of retention timing applies to gas chromatography, except HPLC uses non-volatile liquids.</div><div><br></div><div>- The stronger they adhere to the stationary phase, the higher their retention times.</div><div>- The better they adhere to the mobile phase, the shorter their retention times.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-381d69cd1a65e9968a3e81c7ef28d40a91db82b0.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;BH&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x2F;THF&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reagent]</span>&nbsp;acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing</span> agent that <i>prioritizes</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> first."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b></span>&nbsp;acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing</span> agent that <i>prioritizes</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> first.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If you use <b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b> on a molecule with esters, carboxylic acids, and ketones, it will selectively reduce the carboxylic acid first.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5dcf6c204afbc46604d264b635586c9fc4ad09ee.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b></span>&nbsp;acts as a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reducing"" data-ordinal=""2"">[oxidizing/reducing]</span> agent that <i>prioritizes</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> first."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b></span>&nbsp;acts as a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing</span> agent that <i>prioritizes</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> first.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If you use <b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b> on a molecule with esters, carboxylic acids, and ketones, it will selectively reduce the carboxylic acid first.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5dcf6c204afbc46604d264b635586c9fc4ad09ee.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b></span>&nbsp;acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing</span> agent that <i>prioritizes</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;carboxylic&#x20;acids&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[functional group]</span> first."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b></span>&nbsp;acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reducing</span> agent that <i>prioritizes</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> first.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If you use <b>BH<sub>3</sub>/THF</b> on a molecule with esters, carboxylic acids, and ketones, it will selectively reduce the carboxylic acid first.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5dcf6c204afbc46604d264b635586c9fc4ad09ee.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Aldehydes/ketones</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <u>reactive</u>&nbsp;than amides, carboxylic acids, and esters."	"<b>Aldehydes/ketones</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>reactive</u>&nbsp;than amides, carboxylic acids, and esters.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Aldehydes &amp; ketones will react the fastest if they're all treated with a reactive reagent. This is why they require the most protection (from protecting groups).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-24d9b7f4ecbe49cc6f97d0a3b01eae67b49a632f.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Esters</b></span>&nbsp;can <i>only</i> be <u>reduced</u> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">primary alcohols</span> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""LAH&#x2F;LiAlH&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reducing agent]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Esters</b></span>&nbsp;can <i>only</i> be <u>reduced</u> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">primary alcohols</span> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">LAH/LiAlH<sub>4</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- LAH and LiAlH<sub>4</sub> are the same.</div>- LAH/LiAlH<sub>4</sub> is the only reducing agent <u>strong enough</u> to reduce an ester (since it's not normally very reactive).</div>"
"Compounds that are <i>more</i> <u>stable</u> than other possible products exist in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""higher&#x20;quantities"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in the final mixture."	"Compounds that are <i>more</i> <u>stable</u> than other possible products exist in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">higher quantities</span> in the final mixture.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Major</b> product: predominates in solution.</div><div>- <b>Minor</b> product: does not predominate in solution.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7066afc3085c4bbf5fcea27581841f7b294b97a5.jpg""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The bigger its atomic radius, the better the leaving group.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-468a90fd927c24d392418114b7861d212aa333ad.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1a3a9251508738e221c7f5e7250c7298d77c876f.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Halides</b>&nbsp;<i>larger</i> in size make for better <u>leaving groups</u> because they have <i>more</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""surface&#x20;area"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""stabilization&#x20;of&#x20;the&amp;nbsp&#x3B;negative&#x20;charge"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, while also having less electron overlapping between its bond."	"<b>Halides</b>&nbsp;<i>larger</i> in size make for better <u>leaving groups</u> because they have <i>more</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">surface area</span> for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">stabilization of the&nbsp;negative charge</span>, while also having less electron overlapping between its bond.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The bigger its atomic radius, the better the leaving group.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-468a90fd927c24d392418114b7861d212aa333ad.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1a3a9251508738e221c7f5e7250c7298d77c876f.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Carbanions</b> (negatively charged carbons) are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;better&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[better/worse]</span> <u>nucleophiles</u> than halides, oxygen, or nitrogen."	"<b>Carbanions</b> (negatively charged carbons) are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>better</i></span> <u>nucleophiles</u> than halides, oxygen, or nitrogen.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The negative charge on a carbanion is <u>much less stable</u> - making it highly reactive.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5947814b26b15e63be226d2dbbf38d9be26f70c5.jpg""><div></div></div>"

"<b>S<sub>N</sub>1</b> reactions can <i>sometimes</i> take place with <i>primary</i> <u>leaving groups</u> when they're on an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">allylic double bond</span> position.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<b>S<sub>N</sub>1 </b>reactions can <i>sometimes</i> take place with <i>primary</i> leaving groups when they're on <u>allylic positions</u> due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""resonance&#x20;stabilization"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of the carbocation."	"<b>S<sub>N</sub>1 </b>reactions can <i>sometimes</i> take place with <i>primary</i> leaving groups when they're on <u>allylic positions</u> due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">resonance stabilization</span> of the carbocation.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is just an exception to the rule that S<sub>N</sub>1 reactions only happen on 3* or 2* substrates.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-75db1663b9d3f5a1744470541acffd5cb0e340d7.jpg""><div></div></div>"
"To determine whether an alkene is <b>E</b> or <b>Z</b>, evaluate which side has the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""highest&#x20;&lt;u&gt;priority&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>." <hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Just like naming substituents, go <u>one carbon out at a time</u>.</div><div>- Even though the cyclic ring has a higher molecular weight, COOH has <u>higher priority</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0e6896a8870aa7e51f3ad3c8110c41ee9fb0d8f8.jpg""></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...unlike vacuum distillation, which is performed under a reduced pressure in order to allow the components to reach a quicker boil.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-938d4d7e84fc52cc6d6d53f104f56ce3c466c70b.jpg""></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The more a compound can intramolecular hydrogen bond, the less it can form intermolecular hydrogen bonds.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-cef09653d8dba02754e68e5fd4602b481d5de946.jpg""></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, <b>α-state</b> &amp; <b>β-state</b> refers to the spin directionality of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;protons"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In NMR spectroscopy, <b>α-state</b> &amp; <b>β-state</b> refers to the spin directionality of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">H<sup>+</sup> protons</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>α-state</b>: aligned in the same direction - giving it lower energy.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>β-state</b>: aligned in the opposite direction - giving it higher enegry (like potential energy).</div><div>- When you excite the α-state to the β-state, you can tell the structure of a molecule based on the frequency associated with its transition.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""b73225af35b0d35a7d5f875ed53f9cd8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""f1597ae8c5f72b0e29b6dde0ad734223.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Chromophores&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are compounds that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i><u>absorb</u></i><u>&nbsp;light</u></span>&nbsp;- exciting their electrons from their ground states to their excited states."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Chromophores</b></span> are compounds that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i><u>absorb</u></i><u>&nbsp;light</u></span>&nbsp;- exciting their electrons from their ground states to their excited states.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The wavelengths that <u>do not</u> get absorbed are the wavelengths of the colors we see.</div><div>- Chromophores are different from fluorophores, because fluorophores re-emit the light but with a different wavelength than what it originally absorbed.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3d602148478b8c5a73026d33ddd3b02fa7a95dc2.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Chromophores</b></span> are compounds that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;absorb&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;light&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;- exciting their electrons from their ground states to their excited states."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Chromophores</b></span> are compounds that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i><u>absorb</u></i><u>&nbsp;light</u></span>&nbsp;- exciting their electrons from their ground states to their excited states.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The wavelengths that <u>do not</u> get absorbed are the wavelengths of the colors we see.</div><div>- Chromophores are different from fluorophores, because fluorophores re-emit the light but with a different wavelength than what it originally absorbed.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3d602148478b8c5a73026d33ddd3b02fa7a95dc2.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Capillary action</b>&nbsp;in TLC happens when compounds have <i>higher</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;adhesive&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[cohesive/adhesive]</span> forces than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;cohesive&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[cohesive/adhesive]</span> forces."	"<b>Capillary action</b>&nbsp;in TLC happens when compounds have <i>higher</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>adhesive</u></span> forces than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>cohesive</u></span> forces.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Adhesive forces are forces between the solution and the sides of the tube... allowing it to ""crawl"" up the side.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-044600294a74749cdbb5ecc9e0552085f1a00978.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c2f41b881f88a53a615c059e213af0f6.png""></div></div>"
"In&nbsp;<b>IR spectroscopy</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""carbonyl"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> bond on a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;amide&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> has an absorbance around <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1650 cm<sup>-1</sup></span> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1690 cm<sup>-1</sup></span>."	"In&nbsp;<b>IR spectroscopy</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">carbonyl</span> bond on a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>amide</u></span> has an absorbance around <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1650 cm<sup>-1</sup></span> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1690 cm<sup>-1</sup></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""9066a0ed8d55e9b415ed11f681ea4c3d.png""><br></div><div><img src=""b11200c60a8bd5d9cc2a86ab6e6e4049.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""></div></div>"
"<b>Enantiomers</b> can be <i>separated</i> from each other by the addition of a <u>chiral reagent</u>, which creates <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;new&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;stereocenters"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - converting them into <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""diastereomers"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Enantiomers</b> can be <i>separated</i> from each other by the addition of a <u>chiral reagent</u>, which creates <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>new</i> stereocenters</span> - converting them into <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">diastereomers</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The newly-formed diastereomers can then be separated from each other by methods like distillation or extraction (anything that separates by physical properties).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-de54725dd0b603fc15e8e746df8b29f6e0e3d4c4.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Resolving&#x20;agents&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chiral reagents</u></span>&nbsp;that can be used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>separate</i> enantiomers from each other</span> in mixtures."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Resolving agents</b></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chiral reagents</u></span>&nbsp;that can be used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>separate</i> enantiomers from each other</span> in mixtures.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Resolving agents</b> create new stereocenters - turning the compounds into a pair of <u>diastereomers</u>. These diastereomers can then be separtaed by their physical properties.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-de54725dd0b603fc15e8e746df8b29f6e0e3d4c4.jpg""></div>"
"<b>HPLC</b> should ideally be used to separate <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;small&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[small/large]</span> <u>sample sizes</u> of compounds at a time."	"<b>HPLC</b> should ideally be used to separate <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>small</i></span> <u>sample sizes</u> of compounds at a time.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-8af4c299a01a4369b7f410af41b645fb39375f85.jpg""></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>n+1 rule</b></span>&nbsp;is <i>only</i> applicable for protons on <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;directly&#x20;adjacent&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> carbons, and they <i style="""">cannot</i> be attached to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">oxygen</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">nitrogen</span>."	"In NMR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>n+1 rule</b></span>&nbsp;is <i>only</i> applicable for protons on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>directly adjacent</u></span> carbons, and they <i style="""">cannot</i> be attached to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">oxygen</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">nitrogen</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>a</sub> &amp; H<sub>b</sub> are close enough to the point where they each alter the magnetic field experienced by the other... splitting occurs.</div><div>- n+1 rule determines how many it splits into. For H<sub>b</sub>, there's 1 proton right nearby (n=1), so n+1 means H<sub>b</sub> splits into a <u style=""font-weight: bold;"">doublet</u> (1+1=2).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 1 non-equivalent proton on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon shows as a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""doublet"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> on the spectrum."	"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 1 non-equivalent proton on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon shows as a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">doublet</span> on the spectrum.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Determined by using the n+1 rule, where n is the number of non-equivalent hydrogens on an adjacent atom.</div><div>- When using the n+1 rule, the other non-equivalent protons need to be on <u>directly adjacent carbons</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div>"
"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 2 non-equivalent protons on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon shows as a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""triplet"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> on the spectrum."	"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 2 non-equivalent protons on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon shows as a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">triplet</span> on the spectrum.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Determined by using the n+1 rule, where n is the number of non-equivalent hydrogens on an adjacent atom.</div><div>- When using the n+1 rule, the other non-equivalent protons need to be on <u>directly adjacent carbons</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div>"
"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 3 non-equivalent protons on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon show as a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""quartet"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> on the spectrum."	"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 3 non-equivalent protons on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon show as a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">quartet</span> on the spectrum.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Determined by using the n+1 rule, where n is the number of non-equivalent hydrogens on an adjacent atom.</div><div>- When using the n+1 rule, the other non-equivalent protons need to be on <u>directly adjacent carbons</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div>"
"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 0 non-equivalent protons on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon shows as a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""singlet"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> on the spectrum."	"In <sup>1</sup>H NMR spectroscopy, 0 non-equivalent protons on a <i>directly</i> <i>adjacent</i> carbon shows as a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">singlet</span> on the spectrum.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Determined by using the n+1 rule, where n is the number of non-equivalent hydrogens on an adjacent atom.</div><div>- When using the n+1 rule, the other non-equivalent protons need to be on <u>directly adjacent carbons</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div>"
"Weak bases <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> <u>deprotonate</u> <b>carboxylic acids</b>&nbsp;- only&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> bases can."	"Weak bases <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> <u>deprotonate</u> <b>carboxylic acids</b>&nbsp;- only&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i></span> bases can.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-048f5d205d4c5a72ecd0361cef9224ac13b0eda0.jpg""></div>"
"In <u>mass spectrometry</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;molecular&#x20;ion&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is first formed when the original sample is initially <i>bombarded</i> with electrons."	"In <u>mass spectrometry</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>molecular ion</b></span> is first formed when the original sample is initially <i>bombarded</i> with electrons.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The molecular ion is then subjected to even more fragmentation.</div><div>- The molecular ion <u>does not</u> necessarily always need to be the tallest peak.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-6ac41131f2a296a6c869592e9c6fa9f797a0545b.jpg""><br></div><div><img src=""paste-aeb8369e7bd0b2140bc8f02b6c9237948d1f34e5.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>In <u>mass spectrometry</u>, the x-axis is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""m&#x2F;z&#x20;ratio"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; the y-axis is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""relative&#x20;abundance"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In <u>mass spectrometry</u>, the x-axis is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">m/z ratio</span> &amp; the y-axis is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">relative abundance</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The molecular ion <u>does not</u> necessarily always need to be the tallest peak.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-6ac41131f2a296a6c869592e9c6fa9f797a0545b.jpg""><br></div><div><img src=""paste-aeb8369e7bd0b2140bc8f02b6c9237948d1f34e5.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>In <u>mass spectrometry</u>, the tallest peak is called the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;parent&#x20;peak&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - being the <i>most abundant</i> fragment in the sample.</div>"	"<div>In <u>mass spectrometry</u>, the tallest peak is called the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>parent peak</b></span> - being the <i>most abundant</i> fragment in the sample.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The abundance of the other peaks are set relative to this.<br></div><div>- The molecular ion <u>does not</u> necessarily always need to be the tallest peak.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-6ac41131f2a296a6c869592e9c6fa9f797a0545b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-aeb8369e7bd0b2140bc8f02b6c9237948d1f34e5.jpg""></div></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, H<sup>+</sup> protons in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chemically <i>equivalent</i></u></span>&nbsp;environments exhibit either <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;rotational&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;planar&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> symmetry."	"In NMR spectroscopy, H<sup>+</sup> protons in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chemically <i>equivalent</i></u></span>&nbsp;environments exhibit either <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>rotational</b></span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>planar</b></span> symmetry.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In order for two protons to be considered chemically equivalent, they <u>must</u> have either <b>rotational</b> or <b>planar</b> symmetry.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b7ebaf9e018e8442cae975260c243da892e6c957.jpg""><br></div><div><img src=""paste-f8a4d12bda0d3fc72cb1594ace694ba022a980eb.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, H<sup>+</sup> protons in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;chemically&#x20;&lt;i&gt;equivalent&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;environments exhibit either <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>rotational</b></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>planar</b></span> symmetry."	"In NMR spectroscopy, H<sup>+</sup> protons in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chemically <i>equivalent</i></u></span>&nbsp;environments exhibit either <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>rotational</b></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>planar</b></span> symmetry.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In order for two protons to be considered chemically equivalent, they <u>must</u> have either <b>rotational</b> or <b>planar</b> symmetry.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b7ebaf9e018e8442cae975260c243da892e6c957.jpg""><br></div><div><img src=""paste-f8a4d12bda0d3fc72cb1594ace694ba022a980eb.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Alcohol</b> groups are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <u>polar</u>&nbsp;than esters, aldehydes, or ketones due to their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ability&#x20;to&#x20;hydrogen&#x20;bond"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Alcohol</b> groups are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>polar</u>&nbsp;than esters, aldehydes, or ketones due to their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ability to hydrogen bond</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Alcohols can hydrogen bond with each other in solution (both donor &amp; acceptor group), while the others cannot.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1119034549fb65998338bdcf7eba3c4e7ee6ebc5.jpg""></div>"
"In acid-base extractions, acids and bases dissolved in the organic layer can <i>enter</i> the <u>aqueous layer</u> after <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""getting&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;i&gt;ionized&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In acid-base extractions, acids and bases dissolved in the organic layer can <i>enter</i> the <u>aqueous layer</u> after <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">getting&nbsp;<i>ionized</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Be sure to use strong acids/bases to ionized functional groups like amines or carboxylic acids.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7db58152d7b0d8106dafec21a6fcd8a910ca9908.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Amines</b> and <b>amides</b> can <i>only</i> be ionized by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""acids"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acids/bases]</span>."	"<b>Amines</b> and <b>amides</b> can <i>only</i> be ionized by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">acids</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"Individual <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ</span>-bonds are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <u>stable</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">π</span>-bonds."	"Individual <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ</span>-bonds are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>stable</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">π</span>-bonds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because the p-orbitals of π-bonds experience less overlapping than σ-bonds, so they're less stable.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0f5b074a49b6a5227523c95f2da339a66277ec9f.jpg""></div>"
"Individual <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""σ"" data-ordinal=""2"">[π/σ]</span>-bonds are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>stable</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""π"" data-ordinal=""2"">[π/σ]</span>-bonds."	"Individual <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ</span>-bonds are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>stable</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">π</span>-bonds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because the p-orbitals of π-bonds experience less overlapping than σ-bonds, so they're less stable.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0f5b074a49b6a5227523c95f2da339a66277ec9f.jpg""></div>"
"<b>π-bonds</b> attached to central atoms <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;are&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[are/are not]</span> considered to be their own individual <u>electron domains</u>."	"<b>π-bonds</b> attached to central atoms <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>are not</i></span> considered to be their own individual <u>electron domains</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: <u>carbonyls</u> are considered sp<sup>2</sup>-hybridized (3 electron domains).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-602d6ab2a3a9e5e8a234441245ceb7800536aa4d.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-c657d3efa1c0890907357d62a07e8a620f6e6060.jpg""></div>"
"In IR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""frequency"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>compound's vibrations</u></span> is <i>proportional</i> to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""frequency"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>infared light it absorbed</u></span>."	"In IR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">frequency</span> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>compound's vibrations</u></span> is <i>proportional</i> to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">frequency</span> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>infared light it absorbed</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Using this informaiton, the <b>wavenumber</b> can be generated (which is inversely proportional to frequency).</div><div><br></div><img src=""3c719375f1b75692740ba15c6486b8d7.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"In IR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">frequency</span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;compound&#x27;s&#x20;vibrations&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is <i>proportional</i> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">frequency</span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;infared&#x20;light&#x20;it&#x20;absorbed&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In IR spectroscopy, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">frequency</span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>compound's vibrations</u></span> is <i>proportional</i> to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">frequency</span> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>infared light it absorbed</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Using this informaiton, the <b>wavenumber</b> can be generated (which is inversely proportional to frequency).</div><div><br></div><img src=""3c719375f1b75692740ba15c6486b8d7.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"

"In <u>affinity chromatography</u>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>affinity tags</b></span> are things <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>added</i> to proteins</span>&nbsp;that give them an affinity for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">compounds lining the column</span> (intended to cause them to adhere together).

"In <u>affinity chromatography</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>affinity tags</b></span> are things <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;added&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;proteins"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;that give them an affinity for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""compounds&#x20;lining&#x20;the&#x20;column"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> (intended to cause them to adhere together)." <div>Ex: proteins are giving histidine affinity tags to cause them to adhere to Nickel lining the column.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2f91010f7211de8ef383f84bb9336826.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Hydrophobic interaction chromatography</b></span> separates <u>proteins</u> from each other on the basis of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hydrophobicity"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Hydrophobic interaction chromatography</b></span> separates <u>proteins</u> from each other on the basis of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">hydrophobicity</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Salt concentration</b>&nbsp;is an important factor in determing whether or not the protein binds tightly with the column.</div><div>- <u>High</u> salt concentrations → neutralizes charges on the protein and <u>enhances</u> hydrophobicity (tighter binding).</div><div><div>- <u>Low</u> salt concentrations → restores charges on the protein and <u>decreases</u> hydrophobic interactions (weaker binding).</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7c6e61bbe928e06e49370e443ca670e440295d4e.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Hydrophobic&#x20;interaction&#x20;chromatography&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[chromatography]</span> separates <u>proteins</u> from each other on the basis of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">hydrophobicity</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Hydrophobic interaction chromatography</b></span> separates <u>proteins</u> from each other on the basis of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">hydrophobicity</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Salt concentration</b>&nbsp;is an important factor in determing whether or not the protein binds tightly with the column.</div><div>- <u>High</u> salt concentrations → neutralizes charges on the protein and <u>enhances</u> hydrophobicity (tighter binding).</div><div><div>- <u>Low</u> salt concentrations → restores charges on the protein and <u>decreases</u> hydrophobic interactions (weaker binding).</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7c6e61bbe928e06e49370e443ca670e440295d4e.jpg""></div>"
"In <u>hydrophobic interaction chromatography</u>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> <b>salt solutions</b> are used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">neutralize</span> the charges of the protein - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strengthening</i></span> hydrophobic interactions in the column."	"In <u>hydrophobic interaction chromatography</u>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>salt solutions</b> are used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">neutralize</span> the charges of the protein - <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strengthening</i></span> hydrophobic interactions in the column.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Salt concentration</b>&nbsp;is an important factor in determing whether or not the protein binds tightly with the column.<br></div><div>- <u>High</u> salt concentrations → neutralizes charges on the protein and <u>enhances</u> hydrophobicity (tighter binding).</div><div><div>- <u>Low</u> salt concentrations → restores charges on the protein and <u>decreases</u> hydrophobic interactions (weaker binding).</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7c6e61bbe928e06e49370e443ca670e440295d4e.jpg""></div>"
"In <u>hydrophobic interaction chromatography</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>salt solutions</b> are used to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""neutralize"" data-ordinal=""2"">[restore/neutralize]</span> the charges of the protein - <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strengthening&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weakening/strengthening]</span> hydrophobic interactions in the column."	"In <u>hydrophobic interaction chromatography</u>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>salt solutions</b> are used to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">neutralize</span> the charges of the protein - <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strengthening</i></span> hydrophobic interactions in the column.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Esters&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[functional group]</span>&nbsp;can <i>only</i> be <u>reduced</u> to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">primary alcohols</span> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">LAH/LiAlH<sub>4</sub></span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- LAH and LiAlH<sub>4</sub> are the same.</div>- LAH/LiAlH<sub>4</sub> is the only reducing agent <u>strong enough</u> to reduce an ester (since it's not normally very reactive).</div>"
"<div>Label the hydrogens that would produce <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>separate</i> peaks</span>&nbsp;on an <b>NMR spectrum</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;ce29525f4d76d0bed83cd5c72619fef7e8d2fa17&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-56a583b062bcec374fbece9498ac8ac4659d1b81.jpg""><br>"	"<div>Label the hydrogens that would produce <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>separate</i> peaks</span>&nbsp;on an <b>NMR spectrum</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-ce29525f4d76d0bed83cd5c72619fef7e8d2fa17.jpg""></span><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-56a583b062bcec374fbece9498ac8ac4659d1b81.jpg""><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- These produce separate peaks because they occupy distinct magnetic envirionments.<div><br></div><div>- Chemically equivalent protons → taller peaks.</div><div>- Chemically non-equivalent protons → separate peaks.</div></div>"
"<div>Label the hydrogens that would produce <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;separate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;peaks"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;on an <b>NMR spectrum</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-ce29525f4d76d0bed83cd5c72619fef7e8d2fa17.jpg""></span><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-56a583b062bcec374fbece9498ac8ac4659d1b81.jpg""><br>"	"<div>Label the hydrogens that would produce <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>separate</i> peaks</span>&nbsp;on an <b>NMR spectrum</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-ce29525f4d76d0bed83cd5c72619fef7e8d2fa17.jpg""></span><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-56a583b062bcec374fbece9498ac8ac4659d1b81.jpg""><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- These produce separate peaks because they occupy distinct magnetic envirionments.<div><br></div><div>- Chemically equivalent protons → taller peaks.</div><div>- Chemically non-equivalent protons → separate peaks.</div></div>"
"<div>On an NMR spectrum, why <i>aren't</i> the H<sup>+</sup> protons labeled 1 <u>equivalent</u> to the protons labeled 2?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""The&#x20;protons&#x20;on&#x20;1&#x20;are&#x20;slightly&#x20;further&#x20;away&#x20;from&#x20;NH&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;and&#x20;slightly&#x20;closer&#x20;to&#x20;the&#x20;sulfonyl&#x20;group&#x20;than&#x20;the&#x20;protons&#x20;on&#x20;2&#x2E;&#x20;They&#x20;occupy&#x20;distinct&#x20;magnetic&#x20;environments&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ce29525f4d76d0bed83cd5c72619fef7e8d2fa17.jpg""></div>"	"<div>On an NMR spectrum, why <i>aren't</i> the H<sup>+</sup> protons labeled 1 <u>equivalent</u> to the protons labeled 2?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">The protons on 1 are slightly further away from NH<sub>2</sub>, and slightly closer to the sulfonyl group than the protons on 2. They occupy distinct magnetic environments.</span></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ce29525f4d76d0bed83cd5c72619fef7e8d2fa17.jpg""></div>
