<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"When a solution boils, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;nucleation&#x20;sites&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are the <u>locations</u> in the fluid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">where the bubbles form</span>."	"When a solution boils, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>nucleation sites</b></span> are the <u>locations</u> in the fluid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">where the bubbles form</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Boiling chips</b> create new nucleation sites at the bottom of the flask by increasing surface area - allowing for more uniform boiling.</div><div>- More nucleation sites allows more bubbles to form - reducing surface tension (preventing superheating).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9a6ad166026775134d89154f5a94d22a3e1af29f.jpg""></div>"
"When a solution boils, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>nucleation sites</b></span> are the <u>locations</u> in the fluid <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""where&#x20;the&#x20;bubbles&#x20;form"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"When a solution boils, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>nucleation sites</b></span> are the <u>locations</u> in the fluid <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">where the bubbles form</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Boiling chips</b> create new nucleation sites at the bottom of the flask by increasing surface area - allowing for more uniform boiling.</div><div>- More nucleation sites allows more bubbles to form - reducing surface tension (preventing superheating).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9a6ad166026775134d89154f5a94d22a3e1af29f.jpg""></div>"
"When an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;epoxide&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is nucleophilically <i>attacked</i> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>vicinal diol</u></span> forms."	"When an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>epoxide</b></span>&nbsp;is nucleophilically <i>attacked</i> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>vicinal diol</u></span> forms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The configuration of the carbon that got attacked always undergoes an <u>inversion of configuration</u>&nbsp;(S<sub>N</sub>2 attack).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-024ff181f4000fab836615ba8f9edd47003a2ce4.jpg""></div>"
"When an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>epoxide</b></span>&nbsp;is nucleophilically <i>attacked</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>vicinal diol</u></span> forms."	"When an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>epoxide</b></span>&nbsp;is nucleophilically <i>attacked</i> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>vicinal diol</u></span> forms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The configuration of the carbon that got attacked always undergoes an <u>inversion of configuration</u>&nbsp;(S<sub>N</sub>2 attack).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-024ff181f4000fab836615ba8f9edd47003a2ce4.jpg""></div>"
"When an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>epoxide</b></span>&nbsp;is nucleophilically <i>attacked</i> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;vicinal&#x20;diol&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[product]</span> forms."	"When an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>epoxide</b></span>&nbsp;is nucleophilically <i>attacked</i> by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>vicinal diol</u></span> forms.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The configuration of the carbon that got attacked always undergoes an <u>inversion of configuration</u>&nbsp;(S<sub>N</sub>2 attack).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-024ff181f4000fab836615ba8f9edd47003a2ce4.jpg""></div>"
"In <i>laboratory&nbsp;</i>organic&nbsp;reactions, the most common <b>acetylating agent</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""acetic&#x20;anhydride"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In <i>laboratory&nbsp;</i>organic&nbsp;reactions, the most common <b>acetylating agent</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">acetic anhydride</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Carbonyl is easily attacked by other compounds - allowing for the transferance of an acetyl group.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>- Most common <u>biological</u>&nbsp;acetylating agent → <b>acetyl-CoA</b>.</div><div>- Most common <u>laboratory</u> acetylating agent → <b>acetic anhydride</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-140e82f4088374320a48008c511e1bec664427a0.jpg""><br></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Free&#x20;radicals&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are compounds with an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>odd</i></span> number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>valence electrons</u></span> &amp; a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>incomplete</i></span> octet - leading to high reactivity."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Free radicals</b></span> are compounds with an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>odd</i></span> number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>valence electrons</u></span> &amp; a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>incomplete</i></span> octet - leading to high reactivity.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Their incomplete octet causes them to <u>pull</u> electrons off of other compounds (<b>oxidizing</b> them).</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Free radicals</b></span> are compounds with an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;odd&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[even/odd]</span> number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;valence&#x20;electrons&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;incomplete&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[complete/incomplete]</span> octet - leading to high reactivity."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Free radicals</b></span> are compounds with an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>odd</i></span> number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>valence electrons</u></span> &amp; a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>incomplete</i></span> octet - leading to high reactivity.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Their incomplete octet causes them to <u>pull</u> electrons off of other compounds (<b>oxidizing</b> them).</div>"
"<b>Free radicals</b> cause oxidative stress by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;removing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[donating/removing]</span> <u>electrons</u> to/from other compounds."	"<b>Free radicals</b> cause oxidative stress by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>removing</i></span> <u>electrons</u> to/from other compounds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Free radicals have an <u>incomplete octet</u> - causing them to oxidize other compounds by removing their valence electrons.</div>"
"Relative <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;basicity&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acidity/basicity]</span> of <b>amines</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Secondary 2°</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Primary 1°</span> = <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Tertiary 3°</span></div>"	"Relative <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>basicity</u></span> of <b>amines</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Secondary 2°</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Primary 1°</span> = <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Tertiary 3°</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Secondary amines are the most basic because their other C groups are electron donating groups. Tertiary amines are ""fully occupied"" by C groups, so it can't accept another proton.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b17848e4068503a57a4ee55b7ffa40a87a0f73d1.jpg""></div>"
"Relative <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>basicity</u></span> of <b>amines</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Secondary&#x20;2&#xB0;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Primary&#x20;1&#xB0;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Tertiary&#x20;3&#xB0;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"Relative <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>basicity</u></span> of <b>amines</b>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Secondary 2°</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Primary 1°</span> = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Tertiary 3°</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Secondary amines are the most basic because their other C groups are electron donating groups. Tertiary amines are ""fully occupied"" by C groups, so it can't accept another proton.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b17848e4068503a57a4ee55b7ffa40a87a0f73d1.jpg""></div>"
"<div>To <i>monitor</i> a <u>reaction's rate</u> using <b>UV-VIS spectroscopy</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;tallest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;peak"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> should be monitored since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">most visible absorbance</span>.</div>"	"<div>To <i>monitor</i> a <u>reaction's rate</u> using <b>UV-VIS spectroscopy</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">tallest</i> peak</span> should be monitored since it has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">most visible absorbance</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Doesn't matter if the tallest peak is a reactant or product.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d11a6bfe67897b71a9e7b38865e83c31f5d6ec15.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;catalyst&#x27;s&#x20;peak&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[peak]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">remains <i>constant</i></span> throughout the reaction.<br></div>"	"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>catalyst's peak</b></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">remains <i>constant</i></span> throughout the reaction.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>No catalyst is shown below - only reactants &amp; products.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9a2f0500870ef33acbbdfe8ccef79766e5d95377.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>catalyst's peak</b></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""remains&#x20;&lt;i&gt;constant&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases/constant]</span> throughout the reaction.<br></div>"	"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>catalyst's peak</b></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">remains <i>constant</i></span> throughout the reaction.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>No catalyst is shown below - only reactants &amp; products.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9a2f0500870ef33acbbdfe8ccef79766e5d95377.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;intermediate&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[peak]</span><b>'s peak</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i>, holds constant, then <i>decreases</i></span> throughout the reaction.</div>"	"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediate</b></span><b>'s peak</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i>, holds constant, then <i>decreases</i></span> throughout the reaction.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is true for the presence of intermediates in reactions in general: they're absent at the very beginning and very end, and are relatively constant in the beginning.</div><div>- No intermediate is shown below - only reactants &amp; products.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9a2f0500870ef33acbbdfe8ccef79766e5d95377.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediate</b></span><b>'s peak</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;holds&#x20;constant&#x2C;&#x20;then&#x20;&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases/constant]</span> throughout the reaction.</div>"	"<div>In <u>UV-VIS spectrscopy</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediate</b></span><b>'s peak</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i>, holds constant, then <i>decreases</i></span> throughout the reaction.</div>

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Resolving agents</b></span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;chiral&#x20;reagents&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;that can be used to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;separate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;enantiomers&#x20;from&#x20;each&#x20;other"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in mixtures."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Resolving agents</b></span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chiral reagents</u></span>&nbsp;that can be used to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>separate</i> enantiomers from each other</span> in mixtures.

<div>- <b>Resolving agents</b> create new stereocenters - turning the compounds into a pair of <u>diastereomers</u>. These diastereomers can then be separtaed by their physical properties.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-de54725dd0b603fc15e8e746df8b29f6e0e3d4c4.jpg""></div>"

"A molecule's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;steric&#x20;number&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>number</i> of <u>electron domains</u></span> around its central carbon, which includes <i>only</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ</span>-bonds &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">lone pairs</span>."	"A molecule's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steric number</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>number</i> of <u>electron domains</u></span> around its central carbon, which includes <i>only</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ</span>-bonds &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">lone pairs</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Steric number of sp<sup>2</sup> orbitals = 3.</div><div>- Steric number of sp<sup>3 </sup>orbitals = 4.</div><div>- Steric number of dsp<sup>3</sup> orbitals = 5.</div><div>etc.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-fbcd16ea7aba666e2ae02a342a530119c87e4bd3.jpg""></div>"
"A molecule's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steric number</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;number&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;electron&#x20;domains&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> around its central carbon, which includes <i>only</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""σ"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>-bonds &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""lone&#x20;pairs"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"A molecule's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steric number</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>number</i> of <u>electron domains</u></span> around its central carbon, which includes <i>only</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">σ</span>-bonds &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">lone pairs</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Steric number of sp<sup>2</sup> orbitals = 3.</div><div>- Steric number of sp<sup>3 </sup>orbitals = 4.</div><div>- Steric number of dsp<sup>3</sup> orbitals = 5.</div><div>etc.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-fbcd16ea7aba666e2ae02a342a530119c87e4bd3.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Highly&#x20;&lt;b&gt;volatile&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span> compounds are often added to reactions by being <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">formed <i>within</i> the reaction mixture</span> as opposed to be poured into it, in order to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>avoid evaporation</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Highly <b>volatile</b></span> compounds are often added to reactions by being <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">formed <i>within</i> the reaction mixture</span> as opposed to be poured into it, in order to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>avoid evaporation</u></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- When a compound absorbs light, its electrons become excited &amp; transition to a higher energy state (hence the π → π* transition).</div><div>- When an electron <u>gets excited</u>, it transitions from a<b> π-orbital</b> (bonding MO) → <b>π*-orbital</b> (anti-bonding MO).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-275426f49c4973d5ffa4c9686ff72c4ee22905a1.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Zinc (Zn)</b> acts as a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;reducing&#x20;agent&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[oxidant/reducing]</span>, since it can easily <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;oxidize&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reduce/oxidize]</span> itself into <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Zn&lt;sup&gt;2&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Zinc (Zn)</b> acts as a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>reducing agent</u></span>, since it can easily <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>oxidize</i></span> itself into <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Zn<sup>2+</sup></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Zinc</b> and <b>copper</b> are cofactors on <u>oxidoreductase enzymes</u> for this reason.</div><b><div><b><br></b></div>Zn</b> → Zn<sup>2+</sup>: gets oxidized, acts as a <u>reducing agent</u>.<div><b>Zn<sup>2+</sup></b> → Zn: gets reduced, acts as an <u>oxidizing agent</u>.</div></div>"
"<b>Zinc (II) (Zn<sup>2+</sup>)</b> acts as a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;oxidizing&#x20;agent&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[oxidant/reducing]</span>, since it can easily <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;reduce&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reduce/oxidize]</span> itself into <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Zn"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Zinc (II) (Zn<sup>2+</sup>)</b> acts as a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>oxidizing agent</u></span>, since it can easily <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>reduce</i></span> itself into <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Zn</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Equivalent orbitals</b>: means the s and p orbitals are <u>equivalent</u> to each other (since they're all hybridized).</div><div>- <b>Unhybridized orbitals</b>: orbitals not apart of the hybridization (these are usually seen in sp2 orbitals).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-6774454d55c579c07a07bed61d24d3d290a7d0ff.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<div>- <b>LDA</b>: large bulky base → kinetic product.</div><div>- <b>NaH</b>: small base → thermodynamic product.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3f790090d8d1d6d85fe0ea34b9b71924c5b07544.jpg""></div>"
"In order for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;LDA&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reagent]</span>&nbsp;or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;NaH&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reagent]</span> to form <u>enolates</u> from ketones, there <i>must</i> be a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">free hydrogen</span> on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">α-carbon</span>."	"In order for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>LDA</b></span>&nbsp;or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>NaH</b></span> to form <u>enolates</u> from ketones, there <i>must</i> be a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">free hydrogen</span> on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">α-carbon</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>LDA and NaH are <u>strong bases</u>, so these form enolates by abstracting protons on the α-carbon... facilitating ketone tautomerization.</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>LDA</b>: large bulky base → kinetic product.</div><div>- <b>NaH</b>: small base → thermodynamic product.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-3f790090d8d1d6d85fe0ea34b9b71924c5b07544.jpg""></div>"

"<b>Resonance stabilization</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>acidity</u></span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>basicity</u></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Stabilizing the electron increases acidity but decreases basicity...<div>&nbsp; &nbsp;- Better conjugate base stabilization = better acid.</div><div>&nbsp; &nbsp;- Better electron donation = better base.</div></div>"
"<b>Resonance stabilization</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;acidity&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[acidity/basicity]</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;basicity&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[acidity/basicity]</span>." <div>- Accepting a hydrogen bond requires the atom to have lone pairs (or highly negative character).</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>Top</b> structure: <u>worst</u> at accepting a hydrogen bond (its electrons are being withdrawn).</div><div>- <b>Bottom</b> structure: <u>best</u> at accepting a hydrogen bond (less electron withdraw and more electron donation from the CH<sub>2</sub>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b6a8bc293b3d23e53c126ea81d55511fd98f8b01.jpg""></div>"

"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylic acid</u></span> is treated with <b>R-MgBr</b>, MgBr adds itself to the nucleophilic oxygen - forming <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&#x2D;MgBr"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""alkane"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> byproduct."	"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylic acid</u></span> is treated with <b>R-MgBr</b>, MgBr adds itself to the nucleophilic oxygen - forming <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">O-MgBr</span> &amp; an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkane</span> byproduct.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- R-MgBr normally reduces the carbonyl to an alcohol &amp; adds its alkyl groups to the old carbonyl carbon. Carboxylic acids are exceptions to this.</div><div>- R-MgBr is a strong base. In the case of carboxylic acids, it instead deprotonates the oxygen and adds its MgBr group.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3ed9c583f2226f4fe87d1bc93c1cc676e4dce146.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""9711e2593b4d5af06c0b25b7c2da0961.png""></div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;carboxylic&#x20;acid&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> is treated with <b>R-MgBr</b>, MgBr adds itself to the nucleophilic oxygen - forming <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O-MgBr</span> &amp; an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkane</span> byproduct."	"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carboxylic acid</u></span> is treated with <b>R-MgBr</b>, MgBr adds itself to the nucleophilic oxygen - forming <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O-MgBr</span> &amp; an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">alkane</span> byproduct.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- R-MgBr normally reduces the carbonyl to an alcohol &amp; adds its alkyl groups to the old carbonyl carbon. Carboxylic acids are exceptions to this.</div><div>- R-MgBr is a strong base. In the case of carboxylic acids, it instead deprotonates the oxygen and adds its MgBr group.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-3ed9c583f2226f4fe87d1bc93c1cc676e4dce146.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""9711e2593b4d5af06c0b25b7c2da0961.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Addition&#x2D;elimination&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions where <u>R-groups</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add themselves</i> to π-bonds</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>eject</i> the leaving group</span> - commonly seen when the R-groups of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">esters</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">amides</span> are replaced."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Addition-elimination</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions where <u>R-groups</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add themselves</i> to π-bonds</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>eject</i> the leaving group</span> - commonly seen when the R-groups of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">esters</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">amides</span> are replaced.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Transesterification reactions</b> are a type of addition-elimination reaction (happens specifically on esters).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f6fcdc3d99144320bf1a0add6fa15553defa709c.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Addition-elimination</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions where <u>R-groups</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;add&#x20;themselves&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;π&#x2D;bonds"" data-ordinal=""2"">[step 1]</span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;eject&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;leaving&#x20;group"" data-ordinal=""2"">[step 2]</span> - commonly seen when the R-groups of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">esters</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">amides</span> are replaced."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Addition-elimination</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions where <u>R-groups</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add themselves</i> to π-bonds</span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>eject</i> the leaving group</span> - commonly seen when the R-groups of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">esters</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">amides</span> are replaced.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Transesterification reactions</b> are a type of addition-elimination reaction (happens specifically on esters).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f6fcdc3d99144320bf1a0add6fa15553defa709c.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Addition-elimination</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions where <u>R-groups</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add themselves</i> to π-bonds</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>eject</i> the leaving group</span> - commonly seen when the R-groups of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""esters"" data-ordinal=""3"">[group]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""amides"" data-ordinal=""3"">[group]</span> are replaced."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Addition-elimination</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions where <u>R-groups</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add themselves</i> to π-bonds</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>eject</i> the leaving group</span> - commonly seen when the R-groups of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">esters</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">amides</span> are replaced.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Addition-elimination reaction: R-group adds itself to a π-bond (i.e. carbonyl) &amp; ejects the old leaving group (old O-R).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a6ec46e5505b1d15d974dd4338462e520b5dfdc5.jpg""></div>"

Number the carbons &amp; redraw.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-589d0d8dfd1fd7165aeb9b6668ca3fb370559226.jpg""><br></div></div>"	"Steps to determine the original&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">diketone</span> that <i>led</i> to this <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>cyclic aldol</b></span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">""Split"" the C-C bond between the <u>α-carbon</u> &amp; the <u>OH's carbon</u>.</span></div><div>2. Number the carbons &amp; redraw.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-589d0d8dfd1fd7165aeb9b6668ca3fb370559226.jpg""><br></div></div>

"<div>In an intramolecular esterification, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;alcohol&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> <i>nucleophilically attacks</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;carbonyl&#x20;carbon&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[group]</span> to eventually form a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>lactone</b></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-24c86eb7076e0ef249386be467bb4a493311ab70.jpg"">"	"<div>In an intramolecular esterification, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>alcohol</u></span> <i>nucleophilically attacks</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>carbonyl carbon</u></span> to eventually form a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>lactone</b></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-24c86eb7076e0ef249386be467bb4a493311ab70.jpg"">

"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fluorophores</b></span> are <u>conjugated π systems</u> that emit photons of light with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""4"">[less/more]</span> energy &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;larger&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""4"">[smaller/larger]</span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">wavelengths</span> than what they absorbed, due to a loss of energy as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">heat</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fluorophores</b></span> are <u>conjugated π systems</u> that emit photons of light with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>less</i></span> energy &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>larger</i></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">wavelengths</span> than what they absorbed, due to a loss of energy as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">heat</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Notice how it <u>absorbs</u> at 340 nm but <u>emits</u> at 541 nm.</div><div>- <b>Fluorophores</b> are essentially just compounds capable of <u>fluorescing</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-eb6a4b2011dd00137cd512d1da7ba3650e731119.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-abd45222af1258bd0a42f962632ef0f02c957ff3.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>Diketones</u></span> undergo <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">intramolecular <b>aldol cyclizations</b></span> after being treated with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">strong base</span> - <i>deprotonating</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""α&#x2D;carbon"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span> for it to attack the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;other&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;carbonyl"" data-ordinal=""4"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>Diketones</u></span> undergo <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">intramolecular <b>aldol cyclizations</b></span> after being treated with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">strong base</span> - <i>deprotonating</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4"">α-carbon</span> for it to attack the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4""><i>other</i> carbonyl</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-d40827ed93c8d64becdbf2fcc92b63108014288b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-41e04a35814f647a3ad9892d2010d8ef961532c9.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>On an <b>IR spectrum</b> for a compound that exhibits <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">multiple</i> <u>resonance structures</u></span>, there would be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x20;single"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span> peak(s) that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;averages&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;characteristics"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of its different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">resonance forms</span>.</div>"	"<div>On an <b>IR spectrum</b> for a compound that exhibits <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">multiple</i> <u>resonance structures</u></span>, there would be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 single</span> peak(s) that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>averages</i> the characteristics</span> of its different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">resonance forms</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>For nitrate below, there is not one single resonance structure that predominates - it is an average of each structure's characteristics. An IR spectrum would therefore only show 1 peak with the characteristics of something with 33% N=O character and 66% N-O character.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ZS8V3tuKTA2y7H153b4M_IMG00012.GIF""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""9066a0ed8d55e9b415ed11f681ea4c3d.png""><br></div><div><img src=""b11200c60a8bd5d9cc2a86ab6e6e4049.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>On an <b>IR spectrum</b> for a compound that exhibits <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;multiple&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;resonance&#x20;structures&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, there would be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 single</span> peak(s) that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>averages</i> the characteristics</span> of its different <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""resonance&#x20;forms"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>On an <b>IR spectrum</b> for a compound that exhibits <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">multiple</i> <u>resonance structures</u></span>, there would be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 single</span> peak(s) that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>averages</i> the characteristics</span> of its different <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">resonance forms</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>For nitrate below, there is not one single resonance structure that predominates - it is an average of each structure's characteristics. An IR spectrum would therefore only show 1 peak with the characteristics of something with 33% N=O character and 66% N-O character.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ZS8V3tuKTA2y7H153b4M_IMG00012.GIF""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""9066a0ed8d55e9b415ed11f681ea4c3d.png""><br></div><div><img src=""b11200c60a8bd5d9cc2a86ab6e6e4049.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>On an <b>IR spectrum</b>, nitrate's <i>multiple</i> resonance structures would display as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x20;single"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> peak(s) with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""33&#x25;&#x20;N&#x3D;O"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> character &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""66&#x25;&#x20;N&#x2D;O"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> character.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ZS8V3tuKTA2y7H153b4M_IMG00012.GIF"">"	"<div>On an <b>IR spectrum</b>, nitrate's <i>multiple</i> resonance structures would display as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 single</span> peak(s) with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">33% N=O</span> character &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">66% N-O</span> character.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ZS8V3tuKTA2y7H153b4M_IMG00012.GIF"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 33% and 66% numbers come from the fact that the characteristics of each of the resonance structures would be averaged together to produce the single peak.</div><div>- For nitrate below, there is not one single resonance structure that predominates - it is an average of each structure's characteristics. An IR spectrum would therefore only show 1 peak with the characteristics of something with 33% N=O character and 66% N-O character.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ZS8V3tuKTA2y7H153b4M_IMG00012.GIF""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""9066a0ed8d55e9b415ed11f681ea4c3d.png""><br></div><div><img src=""b11200c60a8bd5d9cc2a86ab6e6e4049.png""><br></div></div>"
"Compounds with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> <u>boiling points</u> exert <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <b>vapor pressures</b>, because a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> temperature is needed for it to accumulate the vapor pressure needed to equal atmospheric pressure." "<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Nucleophilicity</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;electronegativity&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[periodic property]</span> of an atom."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Nucleophilicity</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electronegativity</u></span> of an atom.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This trend primairly holds as you move <u>within the same row</u> on the periodic table (left → right or right → left).</div>- The more electronegative an atom is, the worse it is as a nucleophile.<div>- Ex: methyl sulfide is a better nucleophile than methanol, because sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2c3c94b2c26d1186e6d5b2cb208dfd6431601d3a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Nucleophilicity&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[nucleophilicity/electrophilicity]</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electronegativity</u></span> of an atom."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Nucleophilicity</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electronegativity</u></span> of an atom.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This trend primairly holds as you move <u>within the same row</u> on the periodic table (left → right or right → left).</div>- The more electronegative an atom is, the worse it is as a nucleophile.<div>- Ex: methyl sulfide is a better nucleophile than methanol, because sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2c3c94b2c26d1186e6d5b2cb208dfd6431601d3a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Nucleophilicity</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;inversely&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[directly/inversely]</span> proportional to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electronegativity</u></span> of an atom."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>Nucleophilicity</b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electronegativity</u></span> of an atom.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This trend primairly holds as you move <u>within the same row</u> on the periodic table (left → right or right → left).</div>- The more electronegative an atom is, the worse it is as a nucleophile.<div>- Ex: methyl sulfide is a better nucleophile than methanol, because sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2c3c94b2c26d1186e6d5b2cb208dfd6431601d3a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weaker&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weaker/stronger]</span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaker</i></span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This trend primairly holds as you move <u>within the same row</u> on the periodic table (left → right or right → left).</div>- The more electronegative an atom is, the more it wants to hold on to its electrons (hence why it's bad at donating them to form a bond).<div>- Ex: methyl sulfide is a better nucleophile than methanol, because sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2c3c94b2c26d1186e6d5b2cb208dfd6431601d3a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaker</i></span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaker</i></span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This trend primairly holds as you move <u>within the same row</u> on the periodic table (left → right or right → left).</div>- The more electronegative an atom is, the more it wants to hold on to its electrons (hence why it's bad at donating them to form a bond).<div>- Ex: methyl sulfide is a better nucleophile than methanol, because sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2c3c94b2c26d1186e6d5b2cb208dfd6431601d3a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;stronger&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weaker/stronger]</span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This trend primairly holds as you move <u>within the same row</u> on the periodic table (left → right or right → left).</div>- The more electronegative an atom is, the more it wants to hold on to its electrons (hence why it's bad at donating them to form a bond).<div>- Ex: methyl sulfide is a better nucleophile than methanol, because sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2c3c94b2c26d1186e6d5b2cb208dfd6431601d3a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>Compounds that are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>electronegative</u> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> <b>nucleophiles</b>.<br></div>

"Why is <b>nucleophilicity</b> <i>inversely</i> proportional to <u>electronegativity</u>?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Highly&#x20;electronegative&#x20;atoms&#x20;will&#x20;have&#x20;a&#x20;tendency&#x20;to&#x20;want&#x20;to&#x20;&lt;i&gt;hold&#x20;on&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;their&#x20;electrons&#x20;&#x2D;&#x20;they&#x20;&lt;i&gt;won&#x27;t&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;want&#x20;to&#x20;form&#x20;bonds&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Why is <b>nucleophilicity</b> <i>inversely</i> proportional to <u>electronegativity</u>?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Highly electronegative atoms will have a tendency to want to <i>hold on</i> to their electrons - they <i>won't</i> want to form bonds.</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This trend primairly holds as you move&nbsp;<u>within the same row</u>&nbsp;on the periodic table (left → right or right → left).</div>- The more electronegative an atom is, the more it wants to hold on to its electrons (hence why it's bad at donating them to form a bond).<div>- Ex: methyl sulfide is a better nucleophile than methanol, because sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-2c3c94b2c26d1186e6d5b2cb208dfd6431601d3a.jpg""></div></div>"

"A <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pull-down assay</b></span> is a subset of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">affinity</span> chromatography, except it detects <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;protein&#x2D;protein&#x20;interactions&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> in the column <i>after</i> the initial detection of the protein."	"A <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pull-down assay</b></span> is a subset of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">affinity</span> chromatography, except it detects <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>protein-protein interactions</u></span> in the column <i>after</i> the initial detection of the protein.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pull-down assays</b>: detects protein-protein interactions in an affinity column.</div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QcY3TPJKHZU"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QcY3TPJKHZU</a></div></div><div>- These assays are very similar to affinity chromatography. The only difference lies with the fact that pull-down assays identify protein-protein interactions (green &amp; red below), as opposed to just identifying a protein from a sample (as is the case in affinity chromatography).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-adcbe4a931e54b2df2128007a0c2219ed0f08771.jpg""></div>"
"A <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pull-down assay</b></span> is a subset of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""affinity"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> chromatography, except it detects <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>protein-protein interactions</u></span> in the column <i>after</i> the initial detection of the protein."	"A <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pull-down assay</b></span> is a subset of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">affinity</span> chromatography, except it detects <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>protein-protein interactions</u></span> in the column <i>after</i> the initial detection of the protein.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pull-down assays</b>: detects protein-protein interactions in an affinity column.</div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QcY3TPJKHZU"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QcY3TPJKHZU</a></div></div><div>- These assays are very similar to affinity chromatography. The only difference lies with the fact that pull-down assays identify protein-protein interactions (green &amp; red below), as opposed to just identifying a protein from a sample (as is the case in affinity chromatography).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-adcbe4a931e54b2df2128007a0c2219ed0f08771.jpg""></div>"
"A <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;pull&#x2D;down&#x20;assay&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[technique]</span> is a subset of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">affinity</span> chromatography, except it detects <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>protein-protein interactions</u></span> in the column <i>after</i> the initial detection of the protein."	"A <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pull-down assay</b></span> is a subset of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">affinity</span> chromatography, except it detects <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>protein-protein interactions</u></span> in the column <i>after</i> the initial detection of the protein.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Pull-down assays</b>: detects protein-protein interactions in an affinity column.</div><div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QcY3TPJKHZU"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QcY3TPJKHZU</a></div></div><div>- These assays are very similar to affinity chromatography. "<b>Pull-down assays</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> be used to identify the typical <u>location</u> of a protein in a cell, because they can indicate whether the protein <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">typically interacts with proteins from <i>certain</i> regions</span>.

"When a <u>carboxylic acid</u> &amp; <u>alcohol</u> react to form an <b>ester</b>, the ester's oxygen atom is the oxygen that was apart of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""attacking&#x20;alcohol"" data-ordinal=""1"">[group]</span>."	"When a <u>carboxylic acid</u> &amp; <u>alcohol</u> react to form an <b>ester</b>, the ester's oxygen atom is the oxygen that was apart of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">attacking alcohol</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-c37f315f0df8112a7f5c651e7d33448b7a3b0aa0.jpg""></div>"
"When an aldehyde/ketone is treated with an alcohol in <i>basic</i> conditions to form a <b>hemiacetal</b>, H<sub>2</sub>O <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[is/is not]</span> lost."	"When an aldehyde/ketone is treated with an alcohol in <i>basic</i> conditions to form a <b>hemiacetal</b>, H<sub>2</sub>O <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>is not</i></span> lost.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>2</sub>O is lost when that hemiacetal is turned into an acetal in acidic conditions, though.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9762e34d8dcdf86be3f0ab2c4aaa29a5.png""></div>"
"When a hemiacetal is treated with another alcohol in <i>acidic</i> conditions to form an&nbsp;<b>acetal</b>, H<sub>2</sub>O <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;is&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[is/is not]</span> lost."	"When a hemiacetal is treated with another alcohol in <i>acidic</i> conditions to form an&nbsp;<b>acetal</b>, H<sub>2</sub>O <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>is</i></span> lost.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>2</sub>O is not lost during the formation of the initial hemiacetal, though.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9762e34d8dcdf86be3f0ab2c4aaa29a5.png""></div>"
"Mechanism of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>keto-enol tautomerization</b></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acidic</i></span> conditions:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Ketone&#x27;s&#x20;oxygen&#x20;is&#x20;&lt;i&gt;protonated&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;by&#x20;H&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""π&#x2D;electrons&#x20;move&#x20;onto&#x20;the&#x20;oxygen&#x20;to&#x20;form&#x20;OH&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O&#x20;&lt;i&gt;deprotonates&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;α&#x2D;carbon&#x20;to&#x20;form&#x20;the&#x20;adjacent&#x20;double&#x20;bond&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"Mechanism of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>keto-enol tautomerization</b></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acidic</i></span> conditions:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Ketone's oxygen is <i>protonated</i> by H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup>.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">π-electrons move onto the oxygen to form OH.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O <i>deprotonates</i> the α-carbon to form the adjacent double bond.</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Can act as a good <u>leaving group</u>.</div>"
"In NMR spectroscopy, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>splitting</b></span> occurs when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;non&#x2D;equivalent&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[equivalent/non-equivalent]</span>&nbsp;protons are in <i>close enough proximity</i> to each other that they directly affect each other's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>magnetic environment</u></span>."	"In NMR spectroscopy, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>splitting</b></span> occurs when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>non-equivalent</i></span>&nbsp;protons are in <i>close enough proximity</i> to each other that they directly affect each other's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>magnetic environment</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>H<sub>a</sub> &amp; H<sub>b</sub> are close enough to the point where they each alter the magnetic field experienced by the other... splitting occurs.</div><div>- n+1 rule determines how many it splits into. For H<sub>b</sub>, there's 1 proton right nearby (n=1), so n+1 means H<sub>b</sub> splits into a <b><u>doublet</u></b>&nbsp;(1+1=2).</div><div><br></div><div><div><img src=""18d03fe06f5f929ed0611e4a5e2e5878.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-82c7fae575dad9e777e41973e820d423a6def091.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"In <b>NMR spectroscopy</b>...<div><br></div><div>- Nearby chemically <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equivalent</i></span>&nbsp;H<sup>+</sup> protons cause <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;taller&#x20;peaks&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div><div>- Nearby chemically <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>non-equivalent</i></span> H<sup>+</sup> protons cause <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;splitting&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"In <b>NMR spectroscopy</b>...<div><br></div><div>- Nearby chemically <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equivalent</i></span>&nbsp;H<sup>+</sup> protons cause <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>taller peaks</u></span>.</div><div>- Nearby chemically <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>non-equivalent</i></span> H<sup>+</sup> protons cause <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>splitting</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-1668a187e207df2e2cd9487ddf167e7268c368ea.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Why are <b style="""">secondary</b> <b>amines</b> <i>more</i> <u>basic</u> than tertiary or primary amines?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""On&#x20;a&#x20;secondary&#x20;amine&#x2C;&#x20;the&#x20;other&#x20;carbons&#x20;are&#x20;&lt;u&gt;electron&#x20;donating&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;making&#x20;it&#x20;more&#x20;basic&#x29;&#x2E;&#x20;On&#x20;tertiary&#x20;amines&#x2C;&#x20;they&#x27;re&#x20;&quot;fully&#x20;occupied&quot;&#x20;and&#x20;can&#x20;&lt;i&gt;no&#x20;longer&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;accept&#x20;protons&#x20;&#x28;less&#x20;basic&#x29;&#x2E;&#x20;Primary&#x20;amines&#x20;don&#x27;t&#x20;have&#x20;as&#x20;many&#x20;electron&#x20;donating&#x20;groups&#x20;as&#x20;secondary&#x20;amines&#x2C;&#x20;so&#x20;they&#x27;re&#x20;less&#x20;basic&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Why are <b style="""">secondary</b> <b>amines</b> <i>more</i> <u>basic</u> than tertiary or primary amines?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">On a secondary amine, the other carbons are <u>electron donating</u> (making it more basic). On tertiary amines, they're ""fully occupied"" and can <i>no longer</i> accept protons (less basic). Primary amines don't have as many electron donating groups as secondary amines, so they're less basic.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>2°</b> &gt; 1° = 3°</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b17848e4068503a57a4ee55b7ffa40a87a0f73d1.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Beer&#x27;s&#x20;Law&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[law]</span>, which describes the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;absorbance&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;light"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> as it <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""passes&#x20;&lt;i&gt;through&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;a&#x20;solution"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-695a253b55f082941e156bfa518fc9da1e9d201e.jpg""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Beer's Law</b></span>, which describes the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>absorbance</u> of light</span> as it <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">passes <i>through</i> a solution</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-695a253b55f082941e156bfa518fc9da1e9d201e.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>A</b> = absorbance; <b>ε</b> = molar absorptivity (constant); <b>b</b> = length of light path; <b>C</b> = concentration.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8f0f08769bca972bc0f4a8837d019f17c363ad20.jpg""><br></div></div>"

"<div>According to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Beer&#x27;s&#x20;Law"" data-ordinal=""3"">[law]</span>, a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> in the compound's <b>concentration (C)</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> the <u>absorbance</u> (A) of the light &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span> the&nbsp;<u>transmittance</u>&nbsp;of the light.</div>"	"<div>According to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">Beer's Law</span>, a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> in the compound's <b>concentration (C)</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> the <u>absorbance</u> (A) of the light &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span> the&nbsp;<u>transmittance</u>&nbsp;of the light.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>A</b> = absorbance; <b>ε</b> = molar absorptivity (constant); <b>b</b> = length of light path; <b>C</b> = concentration.</div><div>- Transmittance refers to the amount of light that exits the medium on the other end.</div><div>- Beer's Law can be used in UV-VIS spectroscopy to determine the unknown concentration of the sample.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8f0f08769bca972bc0f4a8837d019f17c363ad20.jpg""><br></div></div>"

"In IR spectroscopy, the <b>wavenumber (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</b> of the light is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;directly&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[directly/inversely]</span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>frequency</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;inversely&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[directly/inversely]</span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>wavelength (λ)</u></span>."	"In IR spectroscopy, the <b>wavenumber (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</b> of the light is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>directly</i></span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>frequency</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>wavelength (λ)</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Inversely proportional to wavelength since wavenumbers are calculated by taking the inverse of wavelength (wavenumber = 1/λ).</div><div>- Used as the units for light absorption in&nbsp;<b>IR spectroscopy</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c6b9164d6d18efa787c26fcaf0eeeccc.png""><br></div></div>"
"In IR spectroscopy, the <b>wavenumber (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</b> of the light is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>directly</i></span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;frequency&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[λ/f]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;wavelength&#x20;&#x28;λ&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[λ/f]</span>."	"In IR spectroscopy, the <b>wavenumber (cm<sup>-1</sup>)</b> of the light is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>directly</i></span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>frequency</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>wavelength (λ)</u></span>.

"Two different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>enantiomers</u></span>&nbsp;produce <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/different]</span> spectr(a/um) in&nbsp;<b>IR spectroscopy</b>."	"Two different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>enantiomers</u></span>&nbsp;produce <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">the <i>same</i></span> spectr(a/um) in&nbsp;<b>IR spectroscopy</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Enantiomers</u> → same IR spectrum, since they have identical physical properties.</div><div>- <u>Diastereomers</u> → different IR spectra, since they have different physical properties.</div><div><b><br></b></div><div><img src=""paste-a9f40f1701fe60df52933d7312a7a078eabeae09.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Enantiomers</u> → same IR spectrum.</div><div>- <u>Diastereomers</u> → different IR spectra.</div><div><b><br></b></div><div><img src=""paste-a9f40f1701fe60df52933d7312a7a078eabeae09.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Bonding</b></span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>more</i> stable &amp; <i>lower</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;electron&#x20;densities&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;lie&#x20;&lt;i&gt;between&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;two&#x20;nuclei"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Bonding</b></span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>more</i> stable &amp; <i>lower</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electron densities</u> lie <i>between</i> the two nuclei</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Bonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density resides between the two nuclei - making it more stable &amp; lower in energy.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Antibonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density lies outside of the two nuclei - making it less stable &amp; higher in energy.</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""0f9af0b773022613f0122fe25f0f74fe.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Bonding&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[bonding/antibonding]</span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>more</i> stable &amp; <i>lower</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electron densities</u> lie <i>between</i> the two nuclei</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Bonding</b></span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>more</i> stable &amp; <i>lower</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electron densities</u> lie <i>between</i> the two nuclei</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Bonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density resides between the two nuclei - making it more stable &amp; lower in energy.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Antibonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density lies outside of the two nuclei - making it less stable &amp; higher in energy.</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""0f9af0b773022613f0122fe25f0f74fe.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Antibonding&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[bonding/antibonding]</span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>less</i> stable &amp; <i>higher</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electron densities</u> lie <i>outside</i>&nbsp;of the two nuclei</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Antibonding</b></span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>less</i> stable &amp; <i>higher</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electron densities</u> lie <i>outside</i>&nbsp;of the two nuclei</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Bonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density resides between the two nuclei - making it more stable &amp; lower in energy.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Antibonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density lies outside of the two nuclei - making it less stable &amp; higher in energy.</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""0f9af0b773022613f0122fe25f0f74fe.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Antibonding</b></span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>less</i> stable &amp; <i>higher</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;electron&#x20;densities&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;lie&#x20;&lt;i&gt;outside&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;of&#x20;the&#x20;two&#x20;nuclei"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Antibonding</b></span> <b>orbitals</b> are <i>less</i> stable &amp; <i>higher</i> in energy because the majority of their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>electron densities</u> lie <i>outside</i>&nbsp;of the two nuclei</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Bonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density resides between the two nuclei - making it more stable &amp; lower in energy.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Antibonding</b>&nbsp;orbitals → most of the electron density lies outside of the two nuclei - making it less stable &amp; higher in energy.</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""0f9af0b773022613f0122fe25f0f74fe.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Strong"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> <b>nucleophiles</b> can be identified by the presence of counter-ions, like Na, Li, or K."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Strong</span> <b>nucleophiles</b> can be identified by the presence of counter-ions, like Na, Li, or K.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

These counter-ions indicate that the anion has a full negative charge (since it's formed an ion with the cation).</div>"
"KI is an example of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> <b>nucleophile</b>, since the <u>K</u><sup>+</sup> acts as the counter-ion while <u>I</u> has a full negative charge."	"KI is an example of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i></span> <b>nucleophile</b>, since the <u>K</u><sup>+</sup> acts as the counter-ion while <u>I</u> has a full negative charge.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Strong nucleophiles usually have counter-ions, like Na, K, or Li.<div>- Weak nucleophiles are usually neutral molecules.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">OH's</span> can be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>converted</i> into Br's</span>&nbsp;through the addition of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>PBr<sub>3</sub></b></span> - acting via an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""S&lt;sub&gt;N&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;2"" data-ordinal=""2"">[S<sub>N</sub>1/S<sub>N</sub>2]</span>&nbsp;mechanism."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">OH's</span> can be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>converted</i> into Br's</span>&nbsp;through the addition of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>PBr<sub>3</sub></b></span> - acting via an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">S<sub>N</sub>2</span>&nbsp;mechanism.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Even though they have lone pairs, they're considered essentially non-polar since they lack the ability to hydrogen bond on their own in solution.</div><div><br></div><img src=""5c43889f105964a02797c1397c16dd10.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Conjugated&#x20;π&#x2D;bonds&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[bonds]</span> <i>easily</i> absorb <b>UV light</b> because there's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">only a <i>small</i> energy difference</span> between their electrons' <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">ground states</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">excited states</span> - making them more easily excitable."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Conjugated π-bonds</u></span> <i>easily</i> absorb <b>UV light</b> because there's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">only a <i>small</i> energy difference</span> between their electrons' <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">ground states</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">excited states</span> - making them more easily excitable.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Base-catalyzed hydrolysis.</div>"
"What would be more suitable for the separation of an <b>amide</b> &amp; excess <b>anhydride</b> from each other in solution: acid-catalyzed or base-catalyzed hydrolysis?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Aqueous&#x20;base&#x2D;catalyzed&#x20;hydrolysis&#x20;&#x28;NaOH&#x28;aq&#x29;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;since&#x20;the&#x20;hydrolysis&#x20;of&#x20;an&#x20;anhydride&#x20;results&#x20;in&#x20;excess&#x20;carboxylic&#x20;acid&#x2E;&#x20;By&#x20;using&#x20;the&#x20;basic&#x20;version&#x2C;&#x20;the&#x20;carboxylic&#x20;acids&#x20;will&#x20;be&#x20;deprotonated&#x20;into&#x20;carboxylate&#x20;ions&#x20;&#x28;COO&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;&#x2C;&#x20;which&#x20;will&#x20;allow&#x20;it&#x20;to&#x20;pass&#x20;into&#x20;the&#x20;aqueous&#x20;phase&#x2E;&#x20;The&#x20;amide&#x20;doesn&#x27;t&#x20;pass&#x20;into&#x20;the&#x20;aqueous&#x20;phase&#x20;as&#x20;well&#x20;as&#x20;the&#x20;carboxylic&#x20;acid&#x2C;&#x20;since&#x20;amides&#x20;are&#x20;less&#x20;reactive&#x20;and&#x20;therefore&#x20;weren&#x27;t&#x20;deprotonated&#x20;by&#x20;the&#x20;NaOH&#x20;&#x28;i&#x2E;e&#x2E;&#x20;didn&#x27;t&#x20;become&#x20;charged&#x20;like&#x20;the&#x20;carboxylate&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"What would be more suitable for the separation of an <b>amide</b> &amp; excess <b>anhydride</b> from each other in solution: acid-catalyzed or base-catalyzed hydrolysis?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Aqueous base-catalyzed hydrolysis (NaOH(aq))</u>, since the hydrolysis of an anhydride results in excess carboxylic acid. By using the basic version, the carboxylic acids will be deprotonated into carboxylate ions (COO<sup>-</sup>), which will allow it to pass into the aqueous phase. The amide doesn't pass into the aqueous phase as well as the carboxylic acid, since amides are less reactive and therefore weren't deprotonated by the NaOH (i.e. didn't become charged like the carboxylate).</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>Nitriles (C≡N)</b>&nbsp;are <i>converted</i> <i>into</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""carboxylic&#x20;acids&#x20;&#x28;COOH&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[group]</span> when hydrolyzed with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">acids (H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup>)</span>."	"<b>Nitriles (C≡N)</b>&nbsp;are <i>converted</i> <i>into</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">carboxylic acids (COOH)</span> when hydrolyzed with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">acids (H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Hydrolyzed with an acid → forms carboxylic acid.</div><div>- Hydrolyzed with a base → forms a carboxylate ion.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f10b35bedc4b4ea4b2825ede1c646820a3442fae.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Nitriles (C≡N)</b>&nbsp;are <i>converted</i> <i>into</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">carboxylic acids (COOH)</span> when hydrolyzed with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""acids&#x20;&#x28;H&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[reagent]</span>."	"<b>Nitriles (C≡N)</b>&nbsp;are <i>converted</i> <i>into</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">carboxylic acids (COOH)</span> when hydrolyzed with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">acids (H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup>)</span>.

"In UV-Vis spectroscopy, <b>cuvettes</b> are designed to have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;the&#x20;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[the same/different]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>optical properties</u></span>&nbsp;as the compound of interest in order to ensure the differences in absorbance between the multiple different cuvettes is due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">compound's absorbance and <i>not</i> the container's</span>."	"In UV-Vis spectroscopy, <b>cuvettes</b> are designed to have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>the same</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>optical properties</u></span>&nbsp;as the compound of interest in order to ensure the differences in absorbance between the multiple different cuvettes is due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">compound's absorbance and <i>not</i> the container's</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Multiple cuvettes are often used with varying known concentrations of the compound in order to determine the concentration of the unknown (using its absorbance). Think of protein assays.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-507c370898285892fd3bb43ecfd3df3559d112d8.jpg""></div>"

"In UV-Vis spectroscopy, <b>cuvettes</b> are designed to have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>the same</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>optical properties</u></span>&nbsp;as the compound of interest in order to ensure the differences in absorbance between the multiple different cuvettes is due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">compound's absorbance and <i>not</i> the container's</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Na&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;CO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">sodium carbonate</span>, and it acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>Bronsted-Lowry base</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub></b></span> is called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">sodium carbonate</span>, and it acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>Bronsted-Lowry base</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub></b></span> is called <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""sodium&#x20;carbonate"" data-ordinal=""2"">[name]</span>, and it acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>Bronsted-Lowry base</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub></b></span> is called <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">sodium carbonate</span>, and it acts as a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>Bronsted-Lowry base</u></span>.

"Alkylation is the process of adding an alkyl group (R-) to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">nitrogen atoms during amine synthesis</span>. Overalkylation occurs when more than one alkyl group is added to nitrogen, leading to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">secondary amines (R2NH) or tertiary amines (R3N)</span> instead of the desired <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""primary&#x20;amine&#x20;&#x28;RNH2&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"Alkylation is the process of adding an alkyl group (R-) to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">nitrogen atoms during amine synthesis</span>. Overalkylation occurs when more than one alkyl group is added to nitrogen, leading to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">secondary amines (R2NH) or tertiary amines (R3N)</span> instead of the desired <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">primary amine (RNH2)</span>.
