Overalkylation occurs when more than one alkyl group is added to nitrogen, leading to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""secondary&#x20;amines&#x20;&#x28;R2NH&#x29;&#x20;or&#x20;tertiary&#x20;amines&#x20;&#x28;R3N&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> instead of the desired <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">primary amine (RNH2)</span>." Overalkylation occurs when more than one alkyl group is added to nitrogen, leading to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">secondary amines (R2NH) or tertiary amines (R3N)</span> instead of the desired <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">primary amine (RNH2)</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>Water </b>acts a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;base&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acid/base]</span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>acidic</u></span> solutions but acts as an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;acid&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acid/base]</span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>basic</u></span> solutions."	"<b>Water </b>acts a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>base</i></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>acidic</u></span> solutions but acts as an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acid</i></span> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>basic</u></span> solutions.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Water can either <i>donate </i>an H<sup>+</sup> (acid) or <i>accept </i>an H<sup>+</sup> (base).</div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Nucleophilic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x2F;&lt;b&gt;electrophilic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[acidbase/nucophilicelectrophilic]</span>&nbsp;reactions favor the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>kinetic</u></span> products of a reaction - whichever product <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">forms the <i>fastest</i></span>.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Nucleophilic</b>/<b>electrophilic</b></span>&nbsp;reactions favor the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>kinetic</u></span> products of a reaction - whichever product <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">forms the <i>fastest</i></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Nucleophilic/electrophilic</b> reactions → favor <u>kinetic</u> products.</div><div>- <b>Acid/base</b> reactions → favor&nbsp;<u>thermodynamic</u> products.</div><div><br></div><img src=""5aac12fc1c699894933423ee7ad06634.png""></div>"
"In order for <b>nucleophilic attacks</b> to happen, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">products</span> <i>must </i>be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;more&#x20;stable&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> than the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reactants</span>."	"In order for <b>nucleophilic attacks</b> to happen, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">products</span> <i>must </i>be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i><u>more stable</u></i></span> than the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reactants</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>Amides </b>are <i>hydrolyzed </i>into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>ammonia (NH</u><sub style="""">3</sub><u>)</u></span> when placed in<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;highly&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;acidic&#x20;or&#x20;basic"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> solutions."	"<b>Amides </b>are <i>hydrolyzed </i>into <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>carboxylic acids</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>ammonia (NH</u><sub style="""">3</sub><u>)</u></span> when placed in<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>highly</i> acidic or basic</span> solutions.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Acidic </b>solution: Carbonyl oxygen gets protonated by the acidic solution, making the carbonyl carbon even more electrophilic (making it easier for H<sub>2</sub>O to attack).</div><div>- Basic solution: OH<sup>-</sup> nucleophilically attacks the carbonyl carbon (as opposed to protonating the carbonyl oxygen).</div><div><br></div><img src=""37b1f68392c84aeb6f3982ed52322191.png""></div>"
